content: A conversation with AI that started with 'does IPv8 have anything to offer?' The answer came in three seconds — no. But what came after made me rethink how protocols evolve, why timing matters, and what the future internet might look like.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Disclaimer The content of this post comes from my conversation with AI (Claude). Claude’s replies may contain factual errors or inaccuracies. However, I think it is still valuable as a starting point for thinking and discussion. Please use your own judgment and verify key information. Note: This is an English translation of the original post. The conversation with Claude was in Mandarin Chinese. Read the original Chinese version here. Today I saw someone post an IPv8 draft on IETF. One quick read was enough to see there was nothing there — weak technical foundation, and it did not actually solve the problems it claimed to solve. I sent it to Claude anyway, partly to confirm my gut, and partly to see what else might come out of the conversation. Three seconds later, Claude agreed: nothing there. The community reaction has been almost entirely negative too. But the interesting part is that the conversation did not stop there. I kept asking follow-up questions: Why did this person submit this proposal? Is there any chance we are all wrong? What if this draft had appeared in the 1990s? Why did IPv6 choose not to be backward compatible? What will the future internet look like? As we talked, I found that even though IPv8 itself is clearly dead on arrival, the conversation itself made me rethink many things — the time window for protocol adoption, the idea that being technically correct does not mean successful deployment, and why the thinking of “one protocol solves everything” is already outdated. Below is the full conversation between me and Claude. I kept Claude’s thinking process and search references. I should also mention that Claude has memory of my past conversations, so its replies sometimes mention things that seem unrelated — like STUNMESH-go, KubeVirt, or EVPN/VXLAN. These are projects and technical areas I work on, and Claude uses them as comparisons and references. I will not add extra comments or notes to the conversation below. Some details may not be fully accurate, but I think the point is not to get every fact perfect — it is to enjoy the process of reading and thinking. Format guide: 🧑 Me is my question 🤖 Claude is Claude’s reply 💭 Thinking Process is Claude’s internal reasoning (expandable) 🔍 Search References are the external sources Claude looked up 🧑 Me: Who proposed the IPv8 draft at the IETF? Does it have real substance? Is IPv8 actually more reasonable than IPv6? 💭 Thinking Process Checking if the IPv8 draft is real and reasonable. The user is asking about the IPv8 IETF draft — who proposed it, whether it is legitimate, and whether it is truly more reasonable than IPv6. This is a factual question about a specific IETF draft. I need to search for information to verify. The user asked in Traditional Chinese, so I will respond in English as instructed. 🔍 Search References: IPv8 IETF draft proposal author Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) draft-thain-ipv8-01 - Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) IPv8 draft proposes backward-compatible IPv4 evolution with … draft-terrell-ip-spec-ipv7-ipv8-addr-cls-04 Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) | Hacker News draft-thain-ipv8-01 IETF has published an IPv8 draft - ~comp - Tildes 🔍 Search References: draft-thain-ipv8 criticism technical review Hacker News IETF提出のIPv8ドラフト、文書の76%が AI生成と判定：専門家が指摘する根本的な設計ミスも判明 Someone proposes twice-as-long IPv8 address scheme, gets slammed by tech pros Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) | Hacker News What Is IPv8? Three Different Things With the Same Name IETF has published an IPv8 draft - ~comp - Tildes 💭 Thinking Process Combining technical details to review the IPv8 draft’s quality. So this is a proposal from Jamie Thain at One Limited in Bermuda, with draft versions from April 2026, but it looks like it’s been largely rejected by experts — GPTZero flagged it as 76% AI-generated content. The key issue is that anyone can submit an IETF draft without working group endorsement or formal approval, and the community on HN and Lobsters generally
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Who is the proposer Jamie Thain is not a random crackpot. He has a real industry background: Former CTO of Bluewave Bermuda, a new ISP in Bermuda using WISP technology Described by peers as “a tech architect with 30 years of experience, managing large-scale operations and budgets” Co-founded InnoFund with Jack Benaim — a tech startup accelerator in Bermuda Currently listed as “One Limited” with email jamie@one.bm, a Bermuda TLD So his background is in telecom operator / ISP CTO roles. He is not a core IETF member, and not a protocol design expert. This explains why the draft’s thinking is very “operator-governance-oriented” rather than “protocol-evolution-oriented.”
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: What problems does he want to solve (motivation) Draft Section 2 lists three core problems: 1. Management Fragmentation His biggest concern is not actually the address space. The draft starts by complaining that DHCP, DNS, NTP, syslog, SNMP, and authentication are all separate systems. There is no shared identity model, no shared authentication mechanism, no shared telemetry format. For ISP operators, this is a real pain point: every time you add a device, you must manually provision many services. Some services require authentication, some do not. Debugging requires correlating logs across multiple systems. 2. Address exhaustion + IPv6 deployment failure His criticism is quite sharp: “IPv6 has been in development for 25 years, and still carries only a small fraction of global traffic. The dual-stack transition model requires every device, application, and network to support two protocols at the same time. This has proven commercially unacceptable. The lack of a forcing function means organizations can keep using CGNAT indefinitely.” This criticism is fair. IPv6 adoption at around 44% is indeed a real fact. 3. BGP routing table explosion + no route ownership validation In 2024, the global BGP4 routing table exceeded 900,000 prefixes and continues to grow. Prefix deaggregation is the main cause — networks announce more specific prefixes for traffic engineering, and the protocol itself has no mechanism to stop this. Also, BGP4 has no binding between what an ASN announces and what it is authorized to announce. So prefix hijacking, route leaks, and bogon injection can all happen. This is also a real pain point. RPKI/ROA exists to solve this. His ten success criteria He also explicitly listed ten requirements (R1 to R10) for a “viable successor,” including: integrated management, single-stack operation, no changes to IPv4 apps, no changes to RFC 1918, no changes to CGNAT, a much larger address space, software-only upgrade with no hardware changes, human-readable addresses (following IPv4 habits), protocol-level enforcement of east-west and north-south security, and a structurally bounded routing table. You can see that he reasoned backward from the perspective of an ISP CTO: “What would a successor that the market could actually accept look like?” His conclusion was: “It must be almost identical to IPv4.” The motivation is understandable. But this is where the problems start.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: How he designed backward compatibility The core design works like this: Address format: 64-bit = r.r.r.r.n.n.n.n r.r.r.r is a 32-bit ASN routing prefix n.n.n.n is a 32-bit host address (same meaning as IPv4) Example: 64496.192.0.2.1 is actually 0.0.251.240.192.0.2.1 The real definition of “IPv4 is a proper subset of IPv8” This is the core trick in his entire backward compatibility story. The rule is: when r.r.r.r = 0.0.0.0, this IPv8 address “is” an IPv4 address, processed using standard IPv4 rules. So 0.0.0.0.192.168.1.1 equals 192.168.1.1. The original IPv4 address space is “embedded” inside the r.r.r.r=0 slice of the IPv8 address space. RFC 1918 addresses stay non-routable. CGNAT devices do not need to change. These claims look reasonable when you start from this definition. Packet header design The new header uses version number 8. It is 8 octets larger than the IPv4 header (the extra space is two 32-bit r.r.r.r extension fields). Structurally, it is just the IPv4 header with source/destination address fields expanded from 32-bit to 64-bit. Socket API compatibility Old apps continue using AF_INET / sockaddr_in. “An IPv8 compatibility layer will transparently intercept socket calls, so applications are completely unaware of IPv8.” New apps can use the new AF_INET8 / sockaddr_in8 (with an extra sin8_asn field). DNS A8 record A new A8 record type stores 64-bit addresses. For IPv4 apps, the resolver returns only the n.n.n.n part, and the stack automatically “prepends r.r.r.r.” Transition mechanisms 8to4 tunnelling: When two IPv8 ASes are separated by a pure IPv4 transit network, IPv8 packets are wrapped in an HTTPS tunnel. The BGP8 8TO4-ENDPOINT attribute automatically carries the IPv4 tunnel endpoint, with zero manual configuration. IPv4 compatibility: An IPv8 border router will “strip the r.r.r.r prefix” when the destination is a pure IPv4 network. Four-phase rollout: Tier 1/2 ISP → Cloud provider → Enterprise → Consumer ISP
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why this compatibility design does not work technically If you have worked with EVPN/VXLAN and KubeVirt, you can probably see the problems immediately. Let me list them: 1. Changing the Version field immediately breaks backward compatibility The first 4 bits of an IPv4 header are the Version field. All existing router ASICs, switch silicon, NIC firmware, host stacks, and firewalls will drop any packet where Version != 4 (or send it to a slow path that then drops it). The draft itself says “IPv8 uses IP version number 8 in the Version field,” but also says “no existing device requires modification.” These two statements cannot both be true at the same time. The claim that “IPv4 is a proper subset” only holds in the special case where r.r.r.r=0.0.0.0. In that case, you are essentially just sending a plain IPv4 packet (Version=4) — it has nothing to do with IPv8. Any real IPv8 packet where r.r.r.r != 0 uses a completely new wire format. IPv4 hardware cannot parse it. 2. Adding 8 octets to the header means you cannot avoid changing NICs/ASICs Modern switch ASIC parsers use fixed pipelines. Header offsets are hard-coded. “Zero modification required isn’t how protocol fields work” — this comment from HN says it exactly. You cannot make a Broadcom Maverick or Trident correctly parse the TCP/UDP header after a Version=8 packet for ECMP hashing. R7 (software-only upgrade, no hardware change) directly contradicts “changing the packet format.” 3. 8to4 tunnelling is essentially dual-stack He says “IPv8 does not need dual-stack,” but then uses an HTTPS tunnel over IPv4-only transit. Running a tunnel means both ends must have an IPv4 stack (for the tunnel endpoints) and an IPv8 stack (inside the tunnel), plus a BGP8 control plane. This is dual-stack with a different name, plus the overhead of encapsulation. R2 is broken. 4. The socket compatibility layer requires kernel changes “Applications are unaware” means the OS kernel must be modified, including the TCP/IP stack, DNS resolver, and socket layer. “No application changes needed” actually pushes all the work to OS vendors (Microsoft, Apple, Linux distros, iOS, Android, and all embedded OSes). R7 is broken again. 5. The model of giving every ASN holder 2^32 addresses will fail This is the most destructive design choice in my view. Every ASN gets 4 billion addresses, which sounds generous. But: There are currently ~120k ASNs globally. 2^32 = 4.2 billion ASNs, so that is still enough. But multihoming breaks immediately. A company that is multi-homed to two ISPs — which ASN’s addresses should it use? The traditional approach is for the company to get PI (Provider Independent) address space. Under this model, the company must get its own ASN to multihome. This model requires roughly one thousand times more ASN allocations than exist today. Address transfer becomes extremely painful. More critically: since addresses are tied to ASNs, anyone who changes providers must renumber their entire organization. IPv6 renumbering is already painful. This is even worse. 6. OAuth at L3 has a chicken-and-egg problem The draft requires “every manageable element to be authorized with an OAuth2 JWT token,” verified by the OAuth8 cache in the local Zone Server. The problem: the device needs a token to talk to the Zone Server, but before it has a token, it cannot do any L3 communication. How does it get a token from the Zone Server in the first place? The draft does not clearly answer this. 7. The Zone Server is a centralization disaster The draft requires every segment to have two Zone Servers (.254/.253), running DHCP8, DNS8, NTP8, NetLog8, OAuth8, WHOIS8 resolver, ACL8, XLATE8, and also serving as the PVRST root. This puts all eggs in one basket. Every end device is also required to maintain a persistent TCP/443 connection to the Zone Server. If you have experience with KubeVirt + Multus, imagine how insane this architecture would be in a cloud-native environment. 8. Requiring PVRST is vendor-specific The draf
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: My overall assessment On the motivation side, the author clearly is genuinely frustrated by the fragmentation of ISP operations. He wants to solve IPv6 stagnation, BGP routing table explosion, BGP hijacking, uncoordinated management interfaces, and east-west security all at once. These are real pain points. You have encountered all of them. But the technical execution is full of contradictions. He wants “full IPv4 compatibility with no hardware changes” AND “a new 64-bit wire format + new socket API + new DNS records + new ARP + new ICMP + mandatory Zone Server + mandatory OAuth2” at the same time. These two goals are physically impossible to achieve together. The result is a document where each chapter looks reasonable on its own, but the whole thing does not hold together. Combined with GPTZero’s finding that it is 76% AI-generated, many sections jump between abstraction levels too quickly. You can see it was assembled through prompt engineering, not by someone who actually sat down and worked through the details. Compared to IPv6, IPv6 is slow to deploy, but it at least has a clear layered design, clear transition tooling (NAT64/DNS64/464XLAT/DS-Lite, etc.), years of interoperability testing, and wide vendor implementation. This IPv8 draft is stuck at the concept paper stage. There is not even a single reference implementation. 🧑 Me: Also do a deep analysis of what problems this draft did not consider. 💭 Thinking Process Systematically reviewing major gaps in the IPv8 draft regarding MTU, IPv6, multicast, cloud, privacy, and routing tables. I’m diving into the IPv8 draft to identify gaps from a network engineering perspective, particularly around areas that would matter to someone with your depth in BGP, EVPN/VXLAN, and Kubernetes networking. Let me work through what the draft overlooks systematically, starting with MTU and fragmentation handling. The 8-octet header expansion alone creates real problems, especially when you layer in 8to4 HTTPS tunneling with TCP, TLS, and HTTPS framing overhead — that’s a serious MTU cliff that the draft completely ignores. PMTUD becomes a nightmare when you’re trying to signal ICMPv8 errors back through an IPv4 HTTPS tunnel, and there’s no discussion of jumbo frames or flow label equivalents either. On the security side, the draft’s approach is fundamentally flawed. WHOIS8 is positioned as critical infrastructure but there’s no bootstrap mechanism, no clarity on who operates it or holds authority, making it a single point of trust failure. The OAuth2 JWT at Layer 3 inherits all the known JWT vulnerabilities — algorithm confusion, key distribution, revocation — without addressing them. There’s no consideration of quantum computing threats to the crypto, no guidance on handling compromised Zone Servers, and the persistent TCP/443 connection from every endpoint to the Zone Server creates an obvious DDoS amplification vector. The privacy implications are actually dystopian. Every DNS8 lookup validates egress, so the Zone Server logs every domain you visit at Layer 3. Combined with OAuth2 tokens identifying each device individually, there’s no privacy extension equivalent, and the whole model breaks Tor, VPNs, and any privacy-preserving technology — it’s essentially mandatory protocol-level surveillance. Mobility is completely absent from the design. If your phone switches from home Wi-Fi at one ASN to cellular at another, what happens to your address binding? There’s no roaming story between Zone Servers at all. For multicast, there’s no PIM equivalent, no SSM support, no IGMP/MLD equivalent, and the inter-ASN multicast approach is just prefix allocation, not an actual routing protocol. The IPv6 problem is massive — nearly half the internet runs IPv6, but IPv8 completely ignores it. There’s no transition path, no 6to8 translation story, and no answer for IPv6-only networks like T-Mobile US. On QoS, they kept IPv4’s TOS field but provided no DSCP semantics, no Flow Label equivalent, no MPLS int
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 1. IPv6 is treated as if it does not exist The full draft mentions IPv6 only two or three times, always as a “failed predecessor.” But the reality is: About 44% of global users have IPv6 connectivity T-Mobile US, Jio, Reliance, and other carriers are primarily IPv6-only + NAT64 AWS, GCP, and Azure use IPv6 heavily in their internal control planes Apple and Google push IPv6-only for mobile networks The draft has no transition story from IPv6 to IPv8 at all. No 6to8, no dual-stack v6 + v8 discussion, no discussion of how to cross IPv6-only transit networks. This means any existing IPv6 deployment that wants to adopt IPv8 must fall back to IPv4. This is commercially impossible.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 2. MTU and encapsulation problems are completely ignored This is especially serious. Let me break it down: IPv8 headers are 8 bytes larger than IPv4 8to4 tunnels use HTTPS, so every IPv8 packet is wrapped in TCP (20) + TLS (~40) + HTTP framing (~30) + IPv4 (20) — about 110+ bytes of overhead Ethernet MTU of 1500, minus all of that, leaves only ~1360 bytes of payload But the draft says nothing about how PMTUD works in a tunnel scenario There is no field similar to the IPv6 Flow Label (which is very important for ECMP hashing) No consideration of Jumbo frames How does ICMPv8 Path MTU Discovery work through an 8to4 HTTPS tunnel? Nothing is written about this If you have done VXLAN MTU tuning, you know this is the most common place things break in practice. The draft barely mentions it.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 3. Multicast and Anycast are theoretical only Multicast: The draft allocates a prefix like ff.ff.00.00/?, but there is no equivalent protocol for PIM (Sparse/Dense/SSM), no replacement for IGMP/MLD, no design for an RP (Rendezvous Point), and no discussion of MSDP for cross-AS multicast. Cross-ASN multicast is extremely difficult in the real world. The draft treats it as just an address allocation problem. Anycast: The draft says “Anycast is not a separate address class in IPv8. Anycast is a routing property implemented via eBGP8. The Cost Factor (CF) metric routes each packet to the nearest BGP8 instance by measured cost automatically.” This completely misunderstands anycast. Today, Cloudflare, Google Public DNS, and root DNS servers use anycast by announcing the same prefix from multiple locations. The /16 minimum injectable prefix rule in IPv8 directly kills all existing anycast deployments. The CF metric measures the cost of a single packet’s path, which is not the same as anycast semantics.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 4. No complete solution for Mobility and Multi-homing Binding addresses to ASNs creates many problems: Phone switching from Wi-Fi to 5G: The ASN changes, so the address changes, and all existing TCP sessions drop. QUIC connection migration works at L4 and cannot save you from an L3 address change. Enterprise multi-homing: The traditional approach is for an enterprise to get its own PI prefix and peer with multiple ISPs. Under the IPv8 model, an enterprise must get its own ASN to multihome. Small and medium businesses will find this very hard. VM live migration: KubeVirt migrates VMs across hosts while keeping the same address. But the draft requires the Zone Server to centrally manage address allocation, plus sticky MAC + switch port OAuth2 binding. Live migration will break immediately. Containers: Pods are created, deleted, and rescheduled dynamically. What is the overhead of ACL8 / OAuth2 re-authentication every time? The draft does not address this.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 5. Cloud and Kubernetes realities are completely ignored This section should be very familiar to you: How CNI works: Cilium, Calico, and Flannel-type CNIs run various solutions on overlay (VXLAN/Geneve) or underlay. The word “overlay” does not appear in the draft at all. VPC model conflict: AWS has millions of customers, each needing an independent address space. If each ASN is bound to 2^32 addresses, AWS needs millions of ASNs — or AWS uses one ASN and divides it among customers (but the draft does not define how). Service Mesh: mTLS and Envoy sidecar L7 traffic management conflicts with the L3 OAuth the draft forces. Serverless: For ephemeral compute like Lambda or Cloud Run, does every cold start need OAuth2 authentication + DHCP8 lease? Kubernetes Service / ClusterIP: There is no discussion at all of how virtual IPs and load balancer IPs work under the IPv8 model. NetworkPolicy: How do K8s L3/L4 policies map to ACL8 / east-west isolation semantics?
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 6. Privacy and surveillance architecture (the scariest part) The IPv8 design is actually a mandatory protocol-level surveillance architecture, but privacy is completely ignored: Every outbound connection must first do a DNS8 lookup: The Zone Server knows every website you visit. Every egress packet is validated against WHOIS8: Connection records are forced to be logged. Every device is identified with an OAuth2 JWT: Every user and every device is tracked at L3. Persistent TCP/443 to Zone Server: Every endpoint keeps a connection open to the central control plane. No anonymization mechanism equivalent to IPv6 Privacy Extensions (RFC 4941/8981) Tor / VPN / STUN tools cannot work at all If you built STUNMESH-go, this last point is especially critical. The entire NAT traversal and P2P architecture depends on “sending packets directly to an IP without first doing DNS.” IPv8 requires no DNS lookup means no XLATE8 state table entry means the connection is blocked. Tools like STUNMESH-go are by design forbidden in an IPv8 network. This is a fatal blow to WireGuard, Tailscale, Syncthing, BitTorrent, and WebRTC P2P. As a side note, this would also be blocked directly by GDPR and various national privacy laws. The draft does not discuss any compliance issues.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 7. DNS is promoted to a critical data plane dependency Today, DNS is control plane. IPv8 makes DNS8 lookup a required step for every outbound connection. The consequences: DNS8 outage = network outage: Today if DNS goes down, you can at least keep existing connections open. Under IPv8, every new connection is blocked. DNSSEC is not mentioned: How does the trust chain integrate with WHOIS8? How do DoH / DoT work: How do third-party DNS resolvers (1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8) coexist with the Zone Server’s DNS8? If third-party DNS cannot be used, that means DNS centralization. Split-horizon DNS and CDN’s EDNS Client Subnet — these common modern DNS practices are completely ignored.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 8. Practical BGP details are all skipped If you know BGP well, these points should really stand out: BGP Communities are not mentioned at all. More than half of modern traffic engineering relies on communities (blackhole community, no-export, AS-prepend requests, etc.). The draft has zero discussion. Route Reflectors vs Confederations — not discussed. BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection): Fast link failure detection, very important in hybrid cloud setups. The draft does not even mention BFD8. BGP Graceful Restart / Graceful Shutdown (RFC 8326): Not mentioned. ASPA (RFC 9715): A more complete path validation mechanism than WHOIS8. The IETF is actively developing it right now. The draft reinvents the wheel. DDoS mitigation via more-specific: When facing a DDoS attack, operators normally announce a more specific prefix to redirect traffic to a scrubbing center. The /16 minimum prefix rule directly forbids this practice. MPLS / Segment Routing / SRv6 — none of these are mentioned, but all global Tier 1 backbones rely on them.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 9. The routing table math does not add up The draft contradicts itself: draft-00 originally said “the BGP8 routing table is limited to one entry per ASN” draft-01 changed this to “/16 minimum injectable prefix,” meaning each ASN can announce multiple /16s But if each ASN can announce multiple /16 subdivisions, the upper limit is 2^16 = 65,536 entries per ASN. With ~120k ASNs globally, the upper limit is 120k × 65k = 7.8 billion entries. Even if only 10% of that is used, it far exceeds today’s 900,000-entry routing table. “Structurally bounded” is just rhetoric, not an actual structural upper bound.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 10. Trust anchor and bootstrap problem The entire system relies on WHOIS8 + OAuth2, but: Who operates WHOIS8: ICANN? RIRs? A new organization? The draft does not define this. How does WHOIS8 bootstrap itself: The WHOIS8 server also needs an IP address and needs to be validated. Chicken-and-egg. What if the OAuth2 provider goes down: The entire authentication system breaks. The draft says “the cache can verify signatures locally,” but JWT revocation is broken in offline mode. Known JWT vulnerabilities: Algorithm confusion (alg=none), key confusion (RS256 vs HS256), replay attacks — the draft mentions none of these defenses. What if a Zone Server is compromised: The entire segment’s trust chain collapses, with no fallback.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 11. Routing protocol details are seriously lacking The CF metric claims to combine seven dimensions — RTT, packet loss, congestion window state, link capacity, and more — into a single 32-bit value. But the weights, normalization methods, and anti-oscillation mechanism (to prevent route flapping) for these seven signals are completely undefined. Why OSPF8 is needed: The draft mandates both OSPF8 and IS-IS8 as options, plus IBGP8, but does not explain why IBGP8 + BFD alone would not be sufficient. IPv8’s OSPF8 is based on OSPFv2 (RFC 2328): But OSPFv2 is an IPv4-only protocol. OSPFv3 is the one that supports flexible address families. The draft chose to base it on OSPFv2 and then extend it, with no explanation for this decision.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 12. No reference implementation and no test vectors This is actually the biggest red flag. Any serious IETF protocol draft normally comes with: A reference implementation (at minimum a Linux kernel patch or a user-space prototype) Interop testing results Test vectors Protocol trace examples IPv8 has ten companion specs (BGP8, IBGP8, OSPF8, IS-IS8, WHOIS8, NetLog8, ARP8, ICMPv8, Route8, MIB8, SNMPv8, WiFi8, Update8, RINE, Zone Server Architecture), but not a single one has any implementation or proof of concept. This is the complete opposite of how IPv6 was developed. IPv6 had implementations first (like the KAME project) and then standardized gradually.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Summary The most fundamental problem with this draft is that it redesigns the network as a centralized management platform for ISP operators. Every “simplified management” claim is built on the assumption of “concentrating all decision-making in the Zone Server.” The result: Losing the end-to-end principle of IP Abandoning the open/permissionless nature of the network layer Forcing protocol-level surveillance Completely conflicting with current cloud-native, P2P, and mobile practices IPv6’s slow adoption is not because “it is not centralized enough.” It is due to deployment costs, insufficient economic incentives, and business model problems. This draft treats the symptom (operational fragmentation) as the disease, and the proposed solution is essentially “make every network into a telecom carrier network.” This goes in the completely opposite direction from the decentralized spirit that made the internet successful over the past 40 years. Given your background presenting STUNMESH-go at FOSDEM 2026, if IPv8 were actually implemented, STUNMESH-go would completely stop working. That is probably the most direct way to feel the impact. 🧑 Me: Give a neutral assessment of what will happen to this draft, and how people’s view of the author will change over time. 🤖 Claude: To be neutral, I will look at “the draft’s outcome” and “how the author’s reputation will change” separately.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: The Draft’s Outcome The near-certain part: This draft will automatically expire on October 17, 2026, and then disappear in the IETF datatracker. This is not a prediction. It is an IETF rule: a personally submitted I-D that does not join a working group within six months automatically expires. Jamie can keep it alive by submitting -02 or -03 versions, but that has no real meaning. No working group will adopt it. For the IETF Internet Area WG or routing area WGs to accept a proposal this large, there must be working group consensus, multiple implementors who commit to building it, and clear alignment with the group’s charter. This draft meets none of these three conditions. It also has over a dozen companion specs and changes that span from L1 to L7. No WG charter can contain all of that. It will not become an RFC. This follows directly from the previous point. The uncertain but possible part: In the next few months, there may be more media coverage. Second-tier tech media like Cybernews have already reported on it, and there may be follow-up articles, especially as the topic of AI-generated RFCs continues to grow. Chinese, Japanese, and Russian communities may publish translated reports. The story will slowly correct from “IETF releases new RFC” to “this is a personal proposal, not an IETF standard.” This event may push some policy discussions inside the IETF. Specifically, a flood of LLM-generated drafts will force the IETF to consider whether to raise submission barriers, require AI-generated labels, or make WG adoption standards stricter. The long-term impact of this event may be more important than the draft itself. The technical legacy left behind is close to zero. The draft contains no original technical insight. The idea of using WHOIS to verify BGP routes has already been solved by RPKI/ROA/ASPA. The need for unified management has already been solved by NETCONF/YANG. BGP security already has BGPsec, RPKI, and ASPA. IPv8 does not advance any technical frontier, so it will not be cited by future research, and it will not inspire new proposals. The outcome in one sentence: It will expire and be forgotten. In a few years, it will become a footnote case for the phenomenon of “AI-generated protocol proposals,” cited alongside IPv9 (the Chinese one from 2004 and various earlier joke versions).
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: How Jamie Thain’s Personal Reputation Will Change This is more complex. It needs to be looked at on several levels. His reputation in the IETF community: In the short term (weeks to months), his reputation in the IETF and network protocol expert community has already been damaged. There are discussions on Hacker News, Lobsters, and IETF mailing lists. People who know the name will remember him as “the person who submitted the AI-generated IPv8.” But this community is actually very small. The number of people globally who seriously follow the IETF may be fewer than a few thousand. In the medium to long term, if he stops submitting related drafts, this event will fade. The network protocol community has a short memory. In three to five years, a new generation of engineers will not remember this. But if he continues to push IPv8 or similarly large proposals, he will be labeled as a “crank,” similar to the type of person described in IETF history as someone who “submits frequently as an individual but never gets adopted by a WG.” His reputation in the local Bermuda and business community: Almost no impact. Local Bermuda news is unlikely to follow this kind of international technical detail. His roles at InnoFund and Bluewave are mainly commercial and ISP operations, not protocol design. His clients have no overlap with the IETF. Wireless Ventures clients care about his practical track record in 4G, FTTH, and submarine cables. That track record will not be affected by one IETF draft. If he packages this as a resume item: This is interesting. Submitting an Internet Draft can be used as a resume highlight in some business contexts. For HR staff, investors, or business clients who are not familiar with how the IETF works, “submitted an IPv8 protocol draft to the IETF” sounds impressive. For his local Bermuda ISP consulting or startup-related work, this is actually a plus. Writing “Author of IETF draft-thain-ipv8-01, proposing the successor to IPv4” on a presentation or pitch deck sounds almost the same as “IETF endorsed” to an audience that does not know the difference. From a practical standpoint, this draft may have a net positive effect on his business reputation, not a net negative one. If the event continues to grow: The key variable is whether the AI-generated draft issue becomes a continuing topic for the IETF or the media. If academic research or IETF policy discussions name this draft as a specific case study, he will be remembered by a wider audience. But even then, what people remember is the negative label of “a representative case of AI slop.” If he personally responds to the criticism, admits that some content was AI-assisted, and makes technical corrections, his reputation may recover somewhat. The IETF community is generally open to people who are “willing to listen to criticism and improve.” But if he insists there is nothing wrong with the draft and continues to push it, his reputation will keep getting worse. So far, I have not found any record of him publicly responding to criticism. The direction is still to be watched. The most likely overall direction: He will be remembered differently by two communities. In the network protocol community, he is “the 2026 AI-generated IPv8 case” with a negative label, but the community is small and the impact is limited. In the Bermuda business community and for general tech media readers, he is still a telecommunications architect with 30 years of experience and co-founder of InnoFund. The IPv8 matter will almost never come up. The estimated impact on his personal career is neutral to slightly positive. The impact on his academic reputation in protocol design circles is negative and irreversible. But the audience for the latter is too small to cause real harm. One point worth reflecting on: This case illustrates the information asymmetry of the modern information environment. Deep technical criticism is limited to professional communities like HN, Lobsters, and IETF mailing lis
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: The Parts That May Genuinely Not Be Problems The “AI-generated” criticism itself may be overrated GPTZero found 76% AI generation, but the inference from this is “so the content is not worth taking seriously.” This logic is flawed. Writing a document with LLM assistance and whether the technical content of the document is correct are two separate questions. Critics are to some extent using an ad hominem attack to smuggle in a technical judgment. If Jamie used an LLM to organize 30 years of ISP operating experience into a formal draft, the value of the content should be judged by the content itself. “No implementation” may be too harsh Most individual drafts in IETF history have no reference implementation. RFCs require implementations, but Internet-Drafts do not. Using this criticism against a draft-00 or -01 version is actually unfair. IPv6 in its draft-00 days was also only a paper design. The centralization criticism may carry ideological bias I said earlier that IPv8 “violates the decentralized spirit of the internet,” but honestly this is a value judgment, not a technical judgment. The modern internet is already highly centralized: Cloudflare proxies a large share of traffic, three major cloud providers dominate, a few DNS resolvers (1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8) handle most queries, and a small number of CAs issue most certificates. IPv8’s Zone Server just writes the centralization that has already happened into the protocol layer. Enterprise networks are even more centralized in practice: Cisco DNA Center, Aruba Central, and Meraki Dashboard are all things like the Zone Server. What the draft does is take the enterprise network governance model and apply it at internet scale. From an enterprise IT perspective, this is unifying a chaotic mix of DHCP, DNS, NTP, SNMP, and syslog into one platform. That is a positive thing. “Breaking P2P” may not be a bug but a feature I emphasized earlier that this would destroy STUN, MESH, WireGuard, BitTorrent, and WebRTC. But most enterprise network administrators would tell you these are things they want to block. BitTorrent traffic, unauthorized VPN, shadow IT, and shadow mesh are headaches for enterprise networks. ISPs also generally dislike P2P traffic because it affects their operating costs. The draft is fundamentally an “enterprise-friendly, ISP-friendly” protocol, not a “user-friendly, developer-friendly” protocol. This is a trade-off, not an error. From the perspective of the Bluewave Bermuda CTO, this trade-off is completely reasonable. “OAuth at L3 chicken-and-egg” is actually solvable I drew that conclusion too quickly earlier. In practice, bootstrap problems are common and all have solutions: EAP-TLS for 802.1X is a similar architecture, using pre-installed certificates to bootstrap DHCP itself is a bootstrap protocol; tokens can be delivered along with the DHCP8 lease Pre-installing OAuth device flow client credentials at the factory is feasible Compared to the provisioning flows already used in automobiles and IoT, this is not particularly difficult. Critics saying “chicken-and-egg” is an instinctive reaction, but technically it is not unsolvable. /16 minimum prefix may be correct BGP deaggregation is a real problem today. Many network operators, especially large transit providers, would welcome this rule. The criticism that “/16 kills anycast and DDoS mitigation” can be read the other way: the current practice actually relies on protocol loopholes. If there were a more structured anycast mechanism (for example, CF metric plus an explicit anycast marker), the long-term situation might be healthier. ASN-bound addresses may simplify many things From a management perspective, the current mix of PI prefixes, PA prefixes, LIR management, and RIR policy is a mess. The model of each ASN getting a fixed /32 host space greatly simplifies address allocation. For ISPs, provisioning new customers does not require applying for new prefixes or worrying about prefix size allocation. This is a real
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: The Criticisms That Still Hold Up After accepting all the steelmanned good-faith readings above, the remaining problems I believe are still real: Wire format changes cannot be absorbed by software upgrades alone This is not an ideological issue. It is a practical physical constraint. Switch ASIC parsers have a fixed pipeline. IPv8’s variable-length header requires hardware replacement. If you have done an EVPN/VXLAN deployment, you know how demanding VTEP hardware offload is. This cannot be faked. The draft’s claim that “software upgrades are enough” while also requiring “wire format extensions” is a logical contradiction. No IPv6 transition story is a fatal flaw The current scale of IPv6 deployment makes it impossible to pretend it does not exist. Any proposal that cannot handle the dual-stack reality has no adoption path in 2026. This is not a problem that good faith reading can fix. ASN-bound addresses will destroy multihoming This is not a value judgment. It is a structural problem. An enterprise that wants to multihome to two ISPs must have its own ASN. This threshold can be defended technically, but in commercial reality, no ISP will simplify the ASN application process enough for small and medium businesses to use. This is an incompatibility between protocol design and institutional design. The scale of companion specs makes adoption impossible Even if we assume every companion spec is technically perfect, simultaneously standardizing 15 specs while requiring hardware, software, operating systems, and the application layer to all upgrade together has never been achieved in the history of any protocol. IPv6 was relatively simple and still took 25 years. IPv8’s packaged approach is practically impossible from the start. Not handling mobility is out of date In 2026, more than half of internet traffic comes from mobile devices. An L3 protocol that cannot handle switching from Wi-Fi to cellular cannot work in the modern world. This is not bias. It is a basic fact.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: A More Balanced Conclusion This draft is a reasonable wish list from an ISP operator’s perspective, written as an impossible technical proposal. The problem list Jamie wants to solve (fragmented management, BGP security, address exhaustion, IPv6 not gaining traction) are all real problems, listed more completely than many network engineers do. Some directions he proposes (WHOIS-verified BGP, integrated management platforms, unified telemetry) are also directions the industry is genuinely moving toward, just solving them with different protocols (RPKI, NETCONF/YANG, OpenConfig, telemetry streaming). The problem is that he bundled everything he wanted to solve into one big bang proposal. If he had split these ideas into separate drafts — “BGP route validation enhancement,” “unified network management suite,” “better east-west security” — each one evaluated on its own would have a better chance of being taken seriously. Bundling them together and labeling it IPv8 is beyond saving, because an IP version bump is the most expensive change in the entire protocol stack. It should not be used to package other improvements. So to answer your original question: yes, if you look at each criticism individually, many can be softened by good-faith reading. But even with all good-faith readings, the strategy itself of packaging everything as an IP version bump plus 15 companion specs is still the fatal mistake. The individual technical details are not all wrong. The strategic choice is wrong. In other words, some individual ideas inside IPv8 are worth discussing. But IPv8 as a proposal for an IP successor has no chance of succeeding in the technical and political environment of 2026. Both judgments can be true at the same time. 🧑 Me: Could this technology eventually become something like MPLS or SRv6, rather than an IP protocol? 🤖 Claude: This is a very insightful question, and I think you have identified a key way to reframe this. I thought about it carefully. The answer has two layers.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why This Repositioning Makes Technical Sense After removing the “IP version bump” packaging from IPv8, what remains actually looks more like an overlay/underlay separation architecture. The dual-layer structure of r.r.r.r / n.n.n.n is essentially a locator/identifier split Look closely at IPv8’s 64-bit address: r.r.r.r is the ASN routing prefix (locator), and n.n.n.n is the host address (identifier). This separation concept is not new at all. LISP (RFC 9300), ILNP, and HIP are all attempts at locator/identifier split. SRv6’s SID also carries similar meaning (SID = locator + function + arguments). If IPv8 is reinterpreted as “SRv6-style 64-bit loc/ID encoding” running in the underlay while still carrying IPv4/IPv6 payload above, many problems disappear: No need to change end hosts, because it is tunnel encapsulation No need to change NIC hardware, because encapsulation is done at the router No need for global coordination, because it can be deployed domain by domain No conflict with IPv6, as they can coexist The “8to4 HTTPS tunnel” already hints at this direction The draft’s own transition solution is a tunnel. Instead of calling it a transition workaround, it would be more honest and more feasible to acknowledge that “this is actually an overlay protocol.” The Zone Server + ASN prefix model corresponds to SD-WAN / SDN controllers If you have done EVPN/VXLAN deployments, you know that the Zone Server architecture is essentially the same as an EVC (EVPN Central) controller, VMware NSX Manager, or Cisco ACI APIC. Reframing IPv8 as “an SD-WAN overlay protocol plus controller architecture” maps it to a product category that already exists in practice. WHOIS8 + BGP8 route validation is converging with RPKI + ASPA This layer is actually already happening. The IETF SIDROPS WG is pushing ASPA, which has a high degree of overlap with what IPv8’s BGP8 validation wants to do. If this part of IPv8 were extracted as a contribution to SIDROPS, it would be easier to accept than as a sub-spec of IPv8.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why It Will Not Evolve Into Something Like SRv6 / MPLS But in practice, I do not think this will happen, for these reasons: SRv6 and MPLS succeeded because they had a clear problem scope and a single focus MPLS solves “doing traffic engineering and VPN on IP networks.” The scope is very clear: label switching, LDP, RSVP-TE, L2VPN, L3VPN. Just those things. SRv6 solves “using IPv6 headers for source routing and network programming.” The scope is also very clear: IPv6 extension headers, SID encoding, SRH, SRv6 policy, uSID. Just those things. IPv8 wants to solve too many things: address space, management integration, BGP security, east-west security, authentication, logging, time synchronization, DNS, update mechanisms, NIC firmware certification, Wi-Fi protocols. To become a successful overlay technology, 80% of the scope must be cut first. Jamie would need to actively give up almost all the marketing points that make the draft look attractive. Without vendor support, it will not follow the MPLS / SRv6 path MPLS was pushed hard by Cisco and Juniper in the late 1990s for the telecom carrier market. SRv6 has been strongly driven by Cisco (leading), Huawei, Nokia, and Arista since 2015, with Linux kernel, VPP, and open source implementations following along. IPv8 has no vendor support at all. An overlay protocol with no hardware vendor, no chip vendor, and no hyperscaler support cannot grow from nothing in the modern world. Modern overlay technologies need ASIC offload (SRv6’s uSID was redesigned specifically to be ASIC-friendly), NIC vendor cooperation (overlay endpoint running on DPUs), and native cloud provider support. Jamie alone cannot clear these barriers. Even if repositioned, more mature solutions already fill every niche Breaking down IPv8: Overlay networking → already have VXLAN, Geneve, SRv6, WireGuard mesh, Tailscale, ZTNA Central management platform → already have Meraki, DNA Center, Aruba Central, NSX, SD-WAN BGP security → already have RPKI, ROA, ASPA, BGPsec Enterprise east-west security → already have micro-segmentation, Zero Trust Network Access, Cilium Network Policy Unified telemetry → already have OpenTelemetry, gNMI, streaming telemetry Identity authentication → already have SPIFFE, OIDC, mTLS Device provisioning → already have ZTP, Ansible, Terraform, DHCPv6 options Every function IPv8 wants to provide already has one or more more mature solutions with established ecosystems. Even if repositioned as an overlay, there is no unfilled niche to enter.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Is There Any Chance Jamie Actually Does This Repositioning Theoretically yes. In practice, the probability is very low. The reason: As a former ISP CTO, his mental model is “solve all problems from the IP protocol layer.” This is not the mindset of an overlay designer. An overlay designer accepts “I cannot control the underlying layer, so I build a layer of VXLAN / Geneve / WireGuard to run my own logic.” An IP protocol designer thinks “let’s change the underlying layer so everyone has to follow.” The text of the draft shows this second mindset throughout. He repeatedly emphasizes “IP version 8,” “IANA assign version number 8,” and “backward compatible with IPv4.” These are the vocabulary of a protocol bump, not the vocabulary of overlay design. If he truly accepts the criticism and repositions, it would become another SRv6 competitor (SRv6-lite? SRv4?). But at that point, he loses the marketing appeal of “IPv4 successor” and loses the level of IETF attention. The incentive for Jamie is not strong enough.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: A More Interesting Possibility: Individual Concepts Get Absorbed in Diluted Form What is more likely to happen is: IPv8 as a complete proposal dies, but some individual concepts may be picked up by other communities. For example: CF (Cost Factor) metric’s seven-signal composite: This idea is actually interesting. BGP’s current decision process relies on coarse metrics like local preference, AS path length, and MED. Adding real measured signals like RTT, loss, capacity, and geographic location has value. But this would go through BGP extended community or IGP extensions, not under the name CF. Unified telemetry format (NetLog8): No need to wait for IPv8. The IETF NETMOD WG and OpenConfig are already doing similar integration. WHOIS as an authoritative source for BGP route validation: This overlaps with the direction of RPKI/ASPA and may be absorbed in other forms. Zone Server’s all-in-one DHCP/DNS/NTP/auth: Enterprise network products are already doing this. Meraki and UniFi already use this model. No new protocol needed. The claim that “east-west security must be enforced at the protocol layer” in enterprise networks: Zero Trust is already moving in this direction, but at the application layer, not L3. So my answer is: Technically, repositioning as an overlay is possible, but organizationally, commercially, and in terms of ecosystem, the probability is close to zero. The more likely outcome is that IPv8 as a whole dies, but a few concepts get absorbed in unrelated forms by other directions in the industry. Your question actually points to a larger pattern: in the 2020s, network protocol evolution almost always goes through the overlay plus SDN controller path. It no longer goes through the IP version bump path. IPv6 was probably the last attempt at a large-scale bump at the IP layer, and it still took 30 years to reach today’s deployment level. IPv8 choosing the same path in 2026 is picking the wrong battlefield from the start. 🧑 Me: From his perspective, why not push IPv6? Why push IPv6? 🤖 Claude: This question requires getting inside Jamie’s head. I will separate “why he does not push IPv6” and “why he might actually push IPv6,” because the answers to both can coexist in the same person.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why He Does Not Push IPv6 1. From an ISP CTO perspective, IPv6 feels like it creates no real work Jamie is CTO of Bluewave in Bermuda. The experience of IPv6 in that environment is completely different from a Silicon Valley hyperscaler engineer. A small ISP doing IPv6 deployment has to: Upgrade all CPE (home routers), and many old devices on the customer side simply do not support it Adjust billing systems (Radius, AAA) Train customer service staff to handle twice as many network issues Manage two sets of firewall rules Keep running CGNAT equipment at the same time For him, IPv6 is “spend a lot of money, customers do not notice, operating costs increase” engineering. The phrase in the draft — “dual-stack transition model proved commercially unacceptable” — is not an abstract criticism. It is his direct personal experience running an ISP. 2. His complaint is not “not enough addresses” but “management is chaotic” Look carefully at draft section 2. Addressing exhaustion is item 2.2, but management fragmentation is 2.1, listed first. This reveals his actual priority of pain points: management fragmentation > BGP routing table > address exhaustion. IPv6 does not solve the first two pain points at all. IPv6 only solves address exhaustion (and very slowly). So from his pain point list, IPv6 is not good enough — not because the technology is bad, but because the problems it solves do not match his most painful problems. 3. IPv6 has features he clearly dislikes Addresses are hard to read: 2001:db8:85a3::8a2e:370:7334 is a nightmare for ISP customer service phone support when people are used to IPv4. The draft’s requirement R8 explicitly requires “Human readable addressing consistent with IPv4 operator familiarity.” This is a direct criticism of IPv6. Hard to remember and hard to say out loud: Reading IPv6 addresses to a customer over the phone is very painful. NDP security problems: IPv6’s NDP has many attack vectors (RA flooding, NS spoofing). He replaced it with ARP8. SLAAC causes ISPs to lose control of address assignment: Autoconfiguration is good for users but bad for ISP management. Extension headers get dropped by many firewalls in the real world: Inconsistent behavior of IPv6 extension headers across transit links is a known nightmare for ISPs. 4. No “centralized control platform” in the design philosophy When IPv6 was designed (in the 1990s), the philosophy was “make endpoints smarter, make the network simpler.” This was a continuation of the end-to-end principle. For ISPs, this philosophy means “we lose control of the network.” What Jamie wants is the opposite: Zone Server central control, OAuth2 authentication for every device, WHOIS8 verification for every route, NetLog8 centralized logging. This is a conflict between telecom operator thinking and native IETF thinking. IPv6 does not have this layer. He had to add it himself, and the result became IPv8. 5. IPv6 is already controlled by existing interest groups This is a real-world political issue. IPv6 promotion already has clear stakeholders: government IPv6 deadlines in various countries, major cloud providers, Cisco and Juniper’s IPv6 feature sets, and RIPE/APNIC IPv6 policy. A Bermuda ISP CTO has no space to contribute in this lane. Inventing IPv8 at least lets him be the “author” in his own new lane. He would never say this motivation out loud, but you can see it from his choice to submit a draft rather than contribute to the v6ops WG.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why He Might Actually Push IPv6 This is the interesting part. If you use good faith to look at his real goals (solving those pain points), the most practical path is actually to start with IPv6, not build IPv8 from scratch. 1. IPv6 already has the address space he wants He wants /32 per ASN = 4 billion addresses. IPv6 gives /32 per site, which is 2^96 addresses. This completely covers his needs and then some. IPv6 wins absolutely on address space. 2. IPv6 has a ready-made deployment ecosystem Linux kernel, BSD stack, Cisco IOS-XR, Juniper JunOS, ASIC hardware, Wireshark, tcpdump, BGP implementations, DNS support (AAAA records). Building IPv8 from scratch would take ten years just to reach IPv6’s current level of tooling. 3. The features he wants can all be added on top of IPv6 Address hierarchy (r.r.r.r / n.n.n.n) → IPv6’s /48 site prefix + /64 subnet + interface ID already has natural hierarchy ASN binding → can be done with RPKI / ASPA on IPv6 Unified management → NETCONF/YANG + OpenConfig are mature on IPv6 East-west security → SRv6 network programming + ACL Route validation → ROA / ASPA / BGPsec Anycast → IPv6 already has complete anycast practices Centralized policy → SDN controller architecture In other words, everything IPv8 wants to do has a solution already available or being developed on top of IPv6. 4. Commercially easier to sell If Jamie really wants to push this vision of “integrated management + security + operations,” packaging it as an “IPv6 best practice bundle” or “IPv6 operator profile” rather than a new protocol would reduce adoption resistance by several orders of magnitude. Small ISPs in Bermuda and even other small ISPs around the world could consider adopting it. 5. The IETF has clear places to contribute v6ops WG: IPv6 operational practice 6man WG: IPv6 maintenance opsawg: operations and management sidrops: BGP security netconf/netmod: management protocols His pain points all have corresponding discussion spaces in these WGs. Even if contributions are not adopted, they at least enter the technical discussion instead of being treated as crank submissions.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why He Did Not Do This This is the most worthwhile question to think about. If the IPv6-based path is clearly more practical, why did he still choose the IPv8 path? My guesses have several layers: Cognitive level: As an ISP CTO, he views IPv6 as “something that failed,” so psychologically he has removed IPv6 from his options. “Adding features on top of a failure” versus “starting over” — the latter intuitively feels more appealing. This is a common “not invented here” bias combined with “IPv6 has failed, so don’t build on it.” Visibility level: Contributing a feature draft to v6ops has a 99% chance of being processed as a normal IETF procedure, with no media attention. Submitting an IPv8 draft has the marketing hook of “IPv4 successor.” Media like Cybernews will report on it. Hacker News will discuss it. It is a huge help for personal visibility, even if it ultimately fails technically. Commercial level: As a co-founder and consultant at InnoFund, “author of IPv8” is a much stronger claim in a business pitch than “contributor to IPv6 operator best practices.” Jamie may well understand that the draft will not succeed, but the draft itself as a personal brand asset has already served its purpose. Psychological level: IPv6 already has thousands of contributors. It is very hard to leave your name on it. IPv8 is yours. Even if it expires, draft-thain-ipv8-01 is permanently listed in the datatracker. The human desire to leave a mark is hard to ignore in situations like this. Philosophical level: He may genuinely believe that IPv6’s end-to-end philosophy is wrong, that the modern internet needs ISP centralized control, and that IPv6 is fundamentally unsuitable for expressing this vision. From this perspective, his IPv8 is a statement of a worldview, not a technical proposal. In that case, an IPv6-based solution cannot replace it, because the point is not the features — the point is the philosophical framework.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: A Deeper Observation The question “why not push IPv6” actually reveals a large pattern in how internet protocols evolve: the technically optimal solution at the IETF and the politically optimal solution for the industry often do not match. IPv6 failed to gain traction for 25 years not because the technology is bad, but because: The side that generates value (end users, app developers) sees no direct benefit The side that pays the cost (ISPs, enterprises) has no strong forcing function The side that truly benefits (IANA, RIRs, future generations freed from address exhaustion) has no commercial leverage Jamie’s IPv8 is in some sense a reaction to this contradiction. He is someone who stands on the ISP side and believes IPv6 put the wrong people in the driver’s seat. What he wants is an ISP-driven, operator-friendly internet architecture. IPv6 does not give him that, so he built IPv8. This frustration is real. But the solution he chose (a completely new IP version plus 15 companion specs) is wrong. If a correct solution exists, it should be an operator-friendly profile built on top of IPv6. In practice, T-Mobile US’s 464XLAT, MAP-T, and IPv6-only with CLAT are moving in that direction. But Jamie did not choose to go that way. So to answer your original question: he does not push IPv6 because of commercial motivations, personal brand concerns, and his mental framework. He should push IPv6 because that is the practical path to actually solving his real pain points. Both answers are correct. One explains his actual behavior. The other describes the theoretically optimal behavior. The gap between them is itself a small portrait of internet protocol politics. 🧑 Me: At this point in time, what is the overall direction of the Hacker News discussion? 💭 Thinking Process I need to search for Hacker News discussions about IPv8 to see what direction the conversation is taking. 🔍 Search References: Web Fetch — Fetched: IPv8 Proposal (Hacker News) (Read full HN discussion thread) 🔍 Search References: Hacker News IPv8 Thain discussion comments vibe-written AI slop Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) | Hacker News Tech pros slam new IPv8 proposal as AI slop Hacker News bans AI-touched comments: broken English is better than slop 🔍 Search References: IPv8 Thain Lobsters IETF mailing list reaction criticism Internet-Draft Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) | Lobsters IETF has published an IPv8 draft - ~comp - Tildes Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) proposal | LowEndTalk В IETF представили драфт спецификации IPv8 / Comments / Habr 💭 Thinking Process Combining the HN discussion context with the community’s overall reaction. I’m seeing the Hacker News discussion coalesce around a few main critiques—people are largely dismissing this as an LLM-generated project without much substance, with comments ranging from skeptical to outright mocking about its legitimacy and timing. The technical objections are more substantive though: the version field change would break existing routers, mixing OAuth into layer 3 feels architecturally wrong, and there’s concern about the centralization and trust assumptions baked into the design. What’s interesting is that tptacek’s clarification about Internet-Drafts being open to anyone seems to be an important reality check for the thread, and chromacity’s take captures a nuanced position—acknowledging that while IPv6’s complexity hurt adoption, embedding application-layer concepts into foundational protocols is generally a bad pattern, even if the addressing scheme itself has merit. The community’s reaction has gotten sharper though: there’s ad hominem dismissal, and the IETF mailing list apparently called it out as AI-generated slop. rocqua’s Chesterton’s fence critique is more thoughtful—framing the author as someone trying to impose coherence without understanding why existing design decisions exist—but the overall engagement is pretty muted at 39 points and 14 comments, suggesting this isn’t resonating as a serious p
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: The size of the discussion already shows the attitude The original thread has only 39 points and 14 comments. For a topic like “a proposal to replace IP,” this is very cold engagement. Compare this to IPv6-only topics, which often get hundreds of points and hundreds of comments. HN’s treatment of IPv8 is basically: “glance at it, laugh, close the tab.” That low engagement is itself a verdict.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Main reaction: Label it as AI slop, do not waste time on deep analysis The top comments are mocking, not technical debate: “The author is listed at a company in Bermuda. Does not look like something from a serious organization. For all I know this might just be something someone vibe-wrote with AI in an afternoon.” “Probably something someone wrote overnight on Adderall and LLM. Completely insane.” “Is this an April Fool’s joke two weeks late?” / “I’m writing an IPv9 proposal where every packet has LLM-verified content. Early deployment required.” The tone means: the HN community did not treat this as a serious technical proposal. The community’s collective judgment is “this is not worth writing a detailed technical rebuttal.” That is actually more damaging than a full technical critique.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: The IETF mailing list reaction is even colder From the IETF mailing list text quoted on Habr: “Regarding thain-ipv8, this has all the characteristics of a very short LLM prompt. This is boring and a waste of everyone’s time.” The IETF mailing list is the territory of protocol professionals. They called it “a waste of everyone’s time.” That is colder than HN’s mockery.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: A few comments with more substance Inside the wave of mockery, a few comments went deeper. These are more valuable than the overall tone: tptacek’s reminder (important): “Obvious reminder: anyone can publish an Internet-Draft.” This is a senior figure in HN’s security community. After he set this tone briefly, the whole thread followed. chromacity’s middle-ground view (rare): “I’m glad I didn’t follow IPv6. But seriously, when we try to put more complex, application-centric logic into foundational protocols, it worries me. The IPv4 and TCP option number assignment tables are a graveyard of experiments. At least back then we had the wisdom to keep those things separate from the main protocol. Putting JWT and OAuth into IP is kind of crazy. I do wish IPv6 had used this addressing scheme but kept everything else. IPv6 expecting to re-architect the entire network slowed adoption.” This comment is the deepest observation in the whole discussion. It acknowledges all three things at once: Putting app-layer logic into L3 is wrong But a 64-bit address space is actually a reasonable idea IPv6’s slow adoption is genuinely because its scope was too large rocqua’s Chesterton’s fence comment: “It feels like a somewhat paternalistic academic who looked deeply at how the network works, saw many different protocols and strange design decisions, and decided: this is not coherent enough. He wants to say, I’ll make the decisions centrally, then it will be coherent. Give each subnet a centralized, trusted, managed source, and the design will be much cleaner. I mean, there’s a lot of nuance and hard-learned lessons in the current state of things, and this design doesn’t seem to have learned from them. It learns some lessons, but I don’t think it passes Chesterton’s fence.” This is a good critique. Chesterton’s fence means “don’t remove something without understanding why it exists.” IPv8 treats the fragmentation in existing protocols as a bug, without realizing it may be a feature (end-to-end principle, decentralization, each layer evolving independently). 19skitsch’s one-sentence summary: “Interesting… feels like a beautiful network designed for a world where operators trust each other more than they actually do.” That one sentence points to IPv8’s trust assumption problem. wg0 and FpUser’s centralization concerns: “At the base level this is a protocol very friendly to censorship.” / “How to make the internet too secure to use, except for government and large corporate websites of course.” HN touched on this angle but did not go deep.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Interesting background context Hacker News updated its community rules in March 2026 to officially ban AI-generated or AI-edited comments. That means the IPv8 draft appeared one month after the HN community had taken a clear anti-AI-slop stance. Community sentiment was at a point of high sensitivity. Anything that looked like an AI product would face especially harsh treatment. LowEndBox published an article with the headline “Vibe-Drafting IETF Proposals is Now a Thing,” treating this event as a landmark case of “AI slop invading the IETF.” Once that framing stuck in the community, the technical content of the draft would never be discussed seriously again.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Lobsters’ reaction Lobsters was even briefer. One top-voted comment: “I got to ’every manageable element in the IPv8 network is authorized by an OAuth2 JWT token’ and noped out. I don’t think one person in Bermuda can single-handedly design the next generation internet protocol.”
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Overall summary The consensus across the professional networking community (HN + Lobsters + IETF mailing list + LowEndTalk) has several layers: First layer (about 80% of reactions): This is AI slop. Not worth taking seriously. Key words: “vibe-written,” “LLM slop,” “April Fool’s,” “waste of time.” Second layer (about 15%): Surface-level technical problems pointed out. Especially: changing the Version field makes zero-modification compatibility impossible, OAuth at L3 is strange, and centralization is friendly to censorship. Third layer (about 5%, most valuable): Deeper architectural criticism. Touching on layering violations, Chesterton’s fence, operator trust assumptions, and how IPv6 should have used a simpler address scheme. But these deep comments were drowned out in volume by the first layer. Collective verdict: Technically DOA (dead on arrival), but the community does not think it deserves a detailed rebuttal. In HN history, if a proposal is treated as a serious opponent, it gets long technical rebuttals. If it only gets mockery and nobody mentions it two weeks later, that is the harshest rejection. IPv8 belongs to the second category. This discussion pattern itself reveals something else: The new problem with AI slop protocol proposals is that they are too bad to be worth rebutting point by point. But “not rebutting” makes non-technical readers think the IETF actually published a new standard. This asymmetry is a new situation the internet protocol world is adapting to in 2026. 🧑 Me: Given that IPv6 was designed from the start to solve the problem once and for all, and it was adopted, shouldn’t the address problem be considered expected? 🤖 Claude: That observation is sharp, and I think you are pointing to a problem that everyone is collectively avoiding. Let me think seriously about this.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Your observation is correct The IPv6 address space design was genuinely well thought out. During the IPng selection period from 1994 to 1998, the IETF was seriously trying to predict “how many devices will exist in the next few decades.” Choosing 128-bit was not a random decision. There were several clear considerations: 1. The real cost of address space layering IPv6’s 128-bit is not meant to hold 2^128 hosts. It is meant to give layered structure plenty of headroom. The design logic was: /48 for each site /64 for each subnet /64 interface ID for SLAAC + EUI-64 A single home user getting a /56 or /48 means one household can divide it into thousands of /64 subnets. This generosity was intentional, to make renumbering, subnet planning, and auto-configuration simpler. 2. Prediction of the IoT explosion Starting in the late 1990s, the IETF was already discussing “internet of things” (the term did not exist yet, but documents like RFC 3819 and RFC 4919 explored sensor networks). The 128-bit space was meant to let every sensor, every appliance, every automotive ECU, every medical device, every industrial PLC have its own globally unique address, with no NAT needed. 3. Prediction of the bureaucratic cost of address allocation Part of IPv4’s address shortage came from “every /32 is a scarce resource,” which created allocation bureaucracy. RIR reviews, justification requirements, reclaiming unused addresses, and a secondary address market. The 128-bit design was meant to make addresses not scarce, eliminating the entire bureaucracy at the root. 4. Long-term use of addresses as identifiers IPsec, Mobile IP, HIP, and loc/ID split all needed addresses to serve as cryptographic identifiers. 128-bit happened to match the crypto key sizes common at the time. So IPv6’s designers were deliberately “over-solving” the address space problem, expecting it would not need to be touched again for 100 years. That judgment now looks correct.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why does IPv8 still have a narrative that “address exhaustion is a problem” Here we need to separate two different kinds of “address exhaustion”: A. Theoretical address space exhaustion: IPv6 fully solves this. No problem. B. Real pain from IPv4 address scarcity in actual deployments: IPv6 has not solved this, because IPv6 adoption is too slow. IPv8’s rhetorical trick is to package B as A. The draft says “address exhaustion is a problem,” and readers think it is talking about address space. But it is actually talking about current IPv4 market prices and the operational pain of CGNAT. These are things IPv6 has solved technically but not in deployment. In other words, IPv6’s design solved the problems it was supposed to solve. But deployment failure itself became a new problem. That new problem is not IPv6’s design fault.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: So where does your observation lead IPv6’s designers’ judgment at the time — “we will solve the address problem completely in one go” — was a correct technical decision. Now in 2026, global IPv6 adoption is at 44%. While not 100%, consider: IPv6 networks that are already deployed truly have no address exhaustion: T-Mobile US, India’s Jio, Google’s internal network, and AWS/Azure/GCP internal networks all use IPv6 heavily, and have never experienced address scarcity. More than 2,000 ASes worldwide run IPv6-only: They connect to the IPv4 world via NAT64/464XLAT. IPv6-only deployment costs are actually lower than dual-stack: T-Mobile US made that judgment years ago. So the “address exhaustion” problem has been solved in environments that already adopted IPv6. The remaining problem is only “people who have not adopted it yet face expensive IPv4 / painful CGNAT.” That is an adoption problem, not a design problem.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: A sharper question So why has IPv6 not reached 100% adoption? That is the real question. And the answer has nothing to do with “how good is IPv6’s address design”: 1. Misaligned commercial incentives: The upgrade cost vs. direct benefit ratio is completely wrong. Those who pay to upgrade (ISPs, enterprises) get no direct benefit. Those who benefit (end users, the global internet) do not pay. This is a classic externality problem, not a technical problem. 2. The IPv4 market is efficient: The secondary address market made IPv4 a tradeable asset. Each /24 sells for $15,000, making it a legitimate investment. This market delayed the forcing function. 3. CGNAT is too effective: ISPs found they could survive without upgrading to IPv6. CGNAT costs less than upgrading the whole network. Technically, CGNAT can support most traffic. 4. Some IPv6 design complexity: Early IPv6 stack implementations were genuinely immature. Managing two sets of configurations in dual-stack is genuinely painful. But these are implementation maturity and deployment experience issues, not design issues. After 2020, these problems were mostly resolved.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Mapping your observation back to evaluating IPv8 Now re-read section 2.2 of the IPv8 draft, “Address Exhaustion”: “IPv6 has been in development for 25 years and still carries only a minority of traffic. The dual-stack transition model has proven commercially unacceptable, and the lack of a forcing function means organizations can continue using CGNAT indefinitely.” This description is technically accurate, but the conclusion “therefore we need IPv8” is wrong. The correct conclusion should be “therefore we need a better IPv6 forcing function.” The US government already has an IPv6 mandate (M-21-07). China is pushing IPv6+. Mobile carriers are leading IPv6-only deployments. IPv4 address prices keep rising (a natural forcing function). All of this is happening. The trend is in the right direction. IPv6 adoption climbed from 5% in 2015 to 44% in 2026. The curve is still going up. Full adoption is only a matter of time, not a matter of design.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Looking at Jamie’s blind spot from another angle Your observation actually reveals Jamie’s core logical error: He treats “IPv6 has not been fully adopted” as evidence that “IPv6’s address design failed.” But those two things are unrelated. The reasons IPv6 has not been fully adopted are economics, transition period challenges, and commercial incentive problems. These cannot be fixed by inventing IPv8.
