Because IPv8 would face exactly the same missing forcing function problem, plus it adds a dozen new problems of its own. The only way to truly solve the “feeling of IPv4 address scarcity” is to complete IPv6 deployment. That path is slow but already underway. Inventing a new protocol just means starting a failed promotion campaign all over again, with no reason to expect IPv8 would succeed faster than IPv6.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: So the answer to your question, “should the address problem be considered expected” From the IETF’s perspective: Yes, completely expected. And it is already solved wherever IPv6 has been adopted. From an ISP CTO’s perspective (Jamie’s viewpoint): No, because he personally feels “my company has not upgraded to IPv6, IPv4 is expensive, and CGNAT is painful.” But the root cause of that pain is not an IPv6 design problem. It is his own, or his ecosystem’s, deployment delay. From a historical judgment perspective: IPv6 is a case of technically correct design but failed deployment strategy. The IPng working group’s choice of 128-bit in the 1990s was correct. But choosing “wire-format incompatible + dual-stack transition” as the deployment strategy was wrong. There is a subtle but important distinction here: address space design (the dimension of the problem) and transition strategy (how to get there) are two different things. IPv6 did the first one well and the second one poorly. What IPv8 tries to do is redo the second part using fake backward compatibility (which actually does not hold), but it also makes no real improvement to the first part (64-bit is actually not enough in an era of an IoT explosion). So in the end, IPv8 makes things worse in the area where IPv6 got it right (address space) and offers a fake solution in the area where IPv6 got it wrong (transition). It is worse than IPv6 on both sides, yet uses IPv6’s failure as its reason for existing. This logic only holds if you completely do not understand IPv6’s design motivations. And that is the core reason why Jamie’s draft reads like “someone who did not do their homework.” Your question made me go back and think about a key point that was not discussed deeply in previous rounds: IPv6’s address space is one of the few long-term decisions the IETF got right. This should be the starting point for any discussion of “an IP successor,” not something to mock as a failure. The IPv8 draft chose the wrong target to criticize from the very beginning. 🧑 Me: What do you think about the reason why many people dislike IPv6 being the hex notation? 🤖 Claude: I think this point is underrated. And when people discuss IPv6 adoption failure, very few take it seriously. Let me give you a straight answer.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: This dislike is real, not ignorance Many in the tech community dismiss “IPv6 is hard to read” as “users are not used to it” or “a training problem.” But if you look carefully, a large portion of the people complaining are experienced engineers who actually understand networking, not beginners. That makes me think it cannot just be a learning curve problem. In that HN thread, sourcegrift wrote: “why cant they do an ipv5 with 64 bits of address space for us humans?” That actually represents the feelings of a certain group of people. Jamie’s draft R8 explicitly writes “Human readable addressing consistent with IPv4 operator familiarity,” which is also a response to this pain. As an ISP CTO, he knows this pain point very well.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why hex notation really does cause practical difficulties 1. Asymmetric cognitive load IPv4’s 192.168.1.1 has each octet from 0 to 255, giving 256^4 = about 4.3 billion combinations. But each octet can be processed as a decimal number in your head. The brain handles decimal automatically, without any conversion. IPv6’s 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334 has each segment as 4 hex digits, covering values from 0 to 65535. The brain has to actively convert hex. Even experienced engineers read it as “two-oh-oh-one, oh-dee-bee-eight” and do not directly feel the numeric value. 2. A disaster for phone calls and verbal communication This is the real pain for ISP customer support. An IPv4 support call: “Your IP is 192.168.1.1, right?” “Yes.” An IPv6 support call: “Your IP is two-zero-zero-one, colon, zero-d-b-eight, colon, eight-five-a-three…” The customer does not know what “a” or “b” means. “d” and “b” are hard to tell apart on the phone. One typo makes the entire address invalid. The “::” abbreviation rule needs to be explained. For someone like Jamie, an ISP CTO, this is a daily scene of watching customer support suffer. It is not an abstract technical problem. 3. Visual scanning / debugging Look at tcpdump or netstat output: IPv4: 192.168.1.100:443 192.168.1.101:443 192.168.1.102:443 At a glance you can see three machines on the same subnet. IPv6: 2001:db8::1:100%eth0]:443 2001:db8::1:101%eth0]:443 2001:db8::2:a0b%eth0]:443 You have to stop and parse which ones share the same prefix. Add the zone index (%eth0), brackets, “::” abbreviation, and hex digits, and the visual parsing cost is high. If you have done Wireshark debugging, you know that IPv6 filter expressions are harder to write and debug, and your eyes get more tired reading logs. 4. Higher error rate when typing by hand 10.0.0.1 vs fe80::1: the latter looks short, but one wrong character makes it invalid. Also, hex keys are spread between a-f and digits, so the typing rhythm is different. The difference in error rates when network operators do manual provisioning is real. 5. IPv6’s ambiguity makes reading and writing harder IPv6 has several valid ways to write the same address: Full: 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001 Leading zeros removed: 2001:db8:0:0:0:0:0:1 Using “::”: 2001:db8::1 RFC 5952 canonical form has additional rules. When you do log analysis, grep, or compare two addresses, you have to normalize them first. IPv4 has no such problem.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: But is hex really a design mistake in IPv6 Thinking seriously: what if IPv6 used dotted decimal? 128-bit in decimal: 32.1.13.184.133.163.0.0.0.0.138.46.3.112.115.52 16 groups of numbers, each from 0 to 255. This is actually harder to read, because 16 groups exceed the visual chunking capacity (human short-term memory typically holds 7±2 chunks). IPv6’s 8 segments already pushes that limit. Or what about hex but with 8-digit segments: 20010db8:85a30000:00008a2e:03707334 4 segments, but each segment requires reading 8 hex characters. The error rate would be higher. The IETF considered this when choosing hex: 8 segments × 4 hex digits = right at the cognitive capacity limit Hex has intuitive bit alignment (4 bits per character) Saves characters when writing The real mistake was not hex, but 128-bit itself. If IPv6 were 64-bit, dotted decimal n.n.n.n.n.n.n.n with 8 segments would work, and it would keep IPv4’s visual habits. That is exactly the choice the IPv8 draft is making.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: So is IPv8’s r.r.r.r.n.n.n.n actually better Partly. Let me separate the points: Parts that are genuinely better: Keeps the cognitive habit of decimal octets ASN dot notation 64496.192.0.2.1 gives the address semantic meaning (you can tell which AS it is just by looking) Human-readable format genuinely helps for ISP support calls, teaching, and debugging No need to deal with the ambiguity of “::” abbreviation rules But it still has problems: 8 octets separated by “.” are fine for a parser, but visually still not as memorable as 4 groups 64496.192.0.2.1 looks like 5-segment IPv4, which is easy to confuse You cannot clearly tell which part is the ASN and which is the host (unlike IPv6’s prefix length conventions) No compression like IPv6’s “::”, so the long form is always 8 segments
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: A more interesting question: did this UX difficulty affect adoption My view is yes, but the tech community has seriously underestimated it. The cost of adopting IPv6 is not just: Buying new equipment Training engineers Updating firewall rules It also includes: Higher customer support costs (reading IPv6 addresses on the phone) Updating documentation (all network admin manuals, new hire training materials) Redesigning ticketing system interfaces Higher difficulty of manually debugging network scripts Resistance to daily operations for small business IT Each of these UX costs is not fatal on its own, but they add up to create adoption inertia. Jamie’s draft implicitly argues: “If IPv6 had used dotted decimal back then, the adoption curve would be different.” That counterfactual cannot be verified, but the intuition is not completely wrong.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: The other side: hex is not actually that bad Let me also be fair to the other argument: 1. Human adaptability exceeds expectations Most network engineers who study IPv6 for three months can already read and write IPv6 addresses fluently. Complaints are mostly concentrated in the early learning period. This is not an inherent barrier, it is a learning curve. 2. Modern tools have already eased many problems DNS means end users almost never have to look at an IP address. Management interfaces (PaloAlto, Fortinet, Cisco) have simplified IPv6 address displays. Modern IDEs and terminals have IPv6 syntax highlighting. Tab completion and copy-paste reduce the need to type by hand. 3. You rarely have to type a full address in practice Enterprise network planning will: Use /48 site prefixes, with only middle bits changing for subnet design. Write short forms like 2001:db8:1:: which is enough. Operate using DNS hostnames. Scenarios that truly require typing a full 128-bit address by hand are rarer than you would think. 4. Hex has advantages for network engineering itself Subnet boundaries align to nibbles (every 4 bits), making prefix calculation easy. For IPv4’s 10.0.0.0/12, you need to convert to find the boundary. For IPv6’s 2001:db8::/36, you can see the position directly from the hex.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: My overall view The point you raised deserves honest acknowledgment: the UX cost of hex notation is real, is non-zero, and has been chronically underestimated in adoption strategy discussions. But it is not the main cause of IPv6 adoption failure. The main cause is economics (the cost to upgrade vs. the direct benefit is misaligned). Hex notation is just one of many friction points. It probably accounts for 5-10% of the total adoption barrier, not 50%. Jamie’s choice of dotted decimal is a correct design intuition, but it is not enough reason to reinvent the entire protocol. More practical approaches would be: Improving the IETF toolchain (ip command output format, RFC 5952 canonical form) Better DNS automation so end users never touch IP addresses Vendor UI improvements These are ten thousand times cheaper than inventing a new protocol. A somewhat counterintuitive observation: The problem of hex being inconvenient is, in some ways, the IETF engineers’ own blind spot. The IETF community is dominated by academics and protocol designers. They do not dislike hex — they actually feel comfortable with it (bit-aligned, easy to calculate). This aesthetic bias meant the IETF did not realize the cognitive advantage of dotted decimal for regular operators. Your question made me think about the lack of UX research in protocol design. For a long time, the IETF has cared a lot about wire format and very little about operator UX. The choice of IPv6 address format is one of the few things that was discussed (RFC 1884, RFC 2373), but the discussion focused on “how to save characters,” not on “the operator’s phone support scenario.” If that kind of user research had been done back then, the design might have been very different. This lesson applies to all future protocol design, not just address format. What engineers find aesthetically pleasing and what causes operators daily pain are often not the same thing. Though IPv8 is overall a failed proposal, the intuition it reflects on this point is actually closer to real-world practice than IPv6’s designers were back then. This is my honest answer to your question: the dislike of hex should not be dismissed. But it is not a savior either. The real barriers to IPv6 adoption are elsewhere. 🧑 Me: If IPv8 had appeared in the 1990s, it could have been discussed. But in the modern era, it has no chance, right? 🤖 Claude: I have thought seriously about this scenario, and the answer is interesting: if IPv8 had appeared in the 1990s, it could have been an IPng candidate and might even have won. But the conditions would have been very different from now. Let me break this down by era.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: The context of the IPng selection period in the 1990s From 1992 to 1995, the IETF was genuinely running a competition to find an IP successor. Three main candidates made it to the final IPng round: 1. SIPP (Simple Internet Protocol Plus) — led by Steve Deering, with 64-bit addresses later expanded to 128-bit, which became today’s IPv6. 2. TUBA (TCP and UDP with Bigger Addresses) — used ISO’s CLNP as its base, following the OSI path. 3. Pip — led by Paul Francis, with variable-length addresses, loc/ID split, and source routing. The final result was SIPP (later named IPv6) winning. But at the time, all three had technical community support. The selection process was not about “the best technology” but involved multiple considerations: existing implementations, vendor support, migration paths, and design philosophy. Key point: At the time there was no clear “winner yet.” Design diversity was accepted.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: How would IPv8’s design elements look in the 1990s If you remove the 2026 AI slop stigma from Jamie’s IPv8 and look at it purely through a 1993 IETF lens, several of its features would actually be discussed seriously: 1. 64-bit addresses + loc/ID split In the 1990s, Paul Francis’s Pip was going in this direction. Discussion of loc/ID split was very active at the IETF then. Nimrod, Pip, and SIP all had this design element. IPv8’s r.r.r.r.n.n.n.n is essentially a simplified loc/ID split. This design would be discussed as a serious proposal in 1993, because: CIDR (RFC 1519, 1993) had not yet fully matured, and prefix allocation models were still being tested. ASN-based routing was a reasonable academic direction. 64-bit was considered sufficient given IoT predictions at the time (the term did not exist yet). 2. Management integration (Zone Server) In the 1990s, enterprise networks still ran Novell IPX/SPX, AppleTalk, DECnet, and IP side by side. “A single network stack covering multiple services” was the normative direction at the time, not an unusual design. Jamie’s Zone Server architecture is similar to Novell NetWare 4.x’s NDS (NetWare Directory Services) + ZENworks management integration model. That was the mainstream market direction in 1994. In that era, it would not appear “overly centralized.” 3. Mandatory authentication (a precursor to OAuth2) In the 1990s, Kerberos was spreading widely (MIT Project Athena, Windows 2000 adopted it). “Authenticate every session” was normal practice in the Kerberos world. If you replaced OAuth2 JWT with a Kerberos ticket, the 1994 IETF would consider this a reasonable design. 4. Human-readable dotted decimal In 1994, when SIPP had just switched to 128-bit hex, there was already internal opposition within the IETF. If IPv8 had come forward with a 64-bit dotted decimal scheme at that time, it might have won support from those who opposed hex. 5. Centralized routing validation (WHOIS8) BGP hijacking had not yet happened at large scale in the 1990s (the first notable incident was AS 7007 in 1997), but routing security was already a discussion topic. At that time, the IETF’s acceptance of “mandatory routing validation” was much higher than it is today.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why IPv8 would still not fully win in the 1990s Even in that era, IPv8 would have had structural problems: 1. Too many companion specs Even in 1993, it would not be realistic to standardize 15 companion specs all at once. IPv6 was simple and still took decades. Standardizing BGP8 + OSPF8 + WHOIS8 + ZoneServer all at the same time would not be possible. 2. No reference implementation In the 1990s, IPng competition had an iron rule: no running code, no voice. Dave Clark’s saying “We reject kings, presidents and voting. We believe in rough consensus and running code” was the motto of that era. SIPP had the NRL implementation, TUBA had several CLNP implementations, and Pip had a Bellcore prototype. If IPv8 only had a spec and no implementation, it would not even qualify to enter the arena. 3. Centralization would still be criticized Even if centralization was not as widely disliked in the 1990s, a Zone Server that every segment was required to have would still be rejected by old-guard IETF figures. David Clark, Jon Postel, and Vint Cerf were all firm supporters of the end-to-end principle at the time. Steve Deering explicitly rejected similar centralization elements when designing IPv6. 4. Binding ASN to addresses would be questioned In the 1990s, multihoming was not mainstream (enterprises connecting to two ISPs was rare). But experts in the IETF’s routing area would quickly point out that this model would destroy multihoming in the future. ASNs had only just begun to be widely used at the time (RFC 1771 for BGP-4 was from 1995). The IETF had not yet settled on “ASN as an identifier,” and Jamie’s approach would trigger a fundamental debate.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: What would happen if Jamie appeared in 1994 Assume the same Jamie, the same draft (replacing OAuth2 JWT with Kerberos, and removing the AI slop parts). His experience in 1994 would have been: Short-term: Entering the IPng directorate for serious discussion, possibly spawning a working group. The technical parts would receive some attention. Medium-term: Facing competition from the SIPP camp, which had more vendor support (DEC, IBM, Sun) and more implementations. IPv8’s Zone Server design would be questioned as “too much like telco, not like internet.” Long-term: Losing to SIPP. But some concepts (loc/ID split, human-readable addresses, integrated management) might have been absorbed into later IPv6 RFCs. Jamie would become a cited minor figure, not someone mocked as a vibe-drafter. The key difference: The 1990s IETF would seriously debate the technical merits and flaws of this draft, even if it ultimately rejected it. The 2026 reaction is “AI slop, not worth discussing.” The difference is not in the technology, but in the era’s level of acceptance.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why there is no chance in 2026 The reasons go deeper than “technology is outdated”: 1. The protocol battle is already over The L3 layer war ended in 1998. The winner is IPv4, the runner-up is IPv6. TUBA, Pip, and all SIPP variants are dead. Entering the L3 competition now is like saying in 1995 “I want to make a new x86 instruction set to replace Intel” — the racing track no longer exists. 2. The innovation battlefield has moved to overlay and application layers After 2010, network innovation has all been in: Overlay (VXLAN, Geneve, WireGuard, SRv6) Application layer (QUIC, HTTP/3) L2 improvements (EVPN, 802.1Q variants) Orchestration (SDN, intent-based networking) L3 itself is seen as already solved. Any L3 proposal will be asked “why not make it an overlay.” 3. The ecosystem lock-in is too deep ASIC hardware: Broadcom, Marvell, and Cisco Silicon One all have 20 years of IPv4/IPv6 optimization. Kernel stacks: Linux, BSD, and Windows networking are all deeply optimized for v4/v6. Network management tools: all monitoring and debugging tools assume v4/v6. Operator training: hundreds of millions of engineers worldwide are trained on v4/v6. RIR policies: the entire address allocation system of APNIC/ARIN/RIPE. This ecosystem lock-in cannot be solved by a good spec. 4. There is already an “unfinished” agenda IPv6 adoption is only at 44%. The IETF and industry are focused on completing the IPv6 transition. Proposing a new protocol in this context is like diverting resources and disrupting the narrative. It is like someone saying in the middle of World War II “let’s start World War III.” 5. Asymmetric attention cost In the 1990s, the number of IETF engineers was small and the number of proposals was small. A serious draft would be read carefully. In 2026, the IETF has thousands of active drafts, limited manpower, and AI slop flooding in. A new proposal first has to prove “it is not AI-generated” before entering substantive discussion. IPv8 did not even pass that threshold. 6. The internet is now critical infrastructure In 1994, the internet was still a mix of research and commerce. Experimental protocols could be discussed. In 2026, the internet is critical infrastructure at the level of the power grid. Any L3 change has huge security and economic consequences. The review cost for a new protocol increases non-linearly.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: A more subtle point Even if IPv8 had a chance of being discussed in the 1990s, it would still be unlikely to beat SIPP/IPv6. The reason is that Steve Deering’s SIPP team was stronger in both politics and engineering execution: Deering was a Stanford PhD, a senior at Xerox PARC, and a familiar face in the IETF. He had backing from Cisco, DEC, IBM, and Sun. Implementation groups (KAME, USAGI) had already started. His design philosophy aligned with the IETF mainstream (end-to-end). In 1994, Jamie was a young engineer from Bermuda who had only been in the workforce for a few years. He did not have that political capital. Even if his technical ideas were reasonable in 1994, he did not have the organizational ability to push IPng. Comparing this to Steve Deering reveals an interesting observation: the success of internet protocols has never been a purely technical competition. It has always been a combination of technology + politics + execution. Jamie is weak on all three. That is true in 1994 or 2026.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: So the answer to your original question In the 1990s, the individual technical ideas in IPv8 would have been discussed seriously. But this package would still not have won and would not have become a standard. Winning required politics + execution + ecosystem, not just good ideas. In 2026, IPv8 does not even have a chance to be discussed seriously. Not because the technology is worse, but because: The L3 battlefield is already closed. The ecosystem lock-in is too deep. AI slop has polluted the proposal signal. The review threshold for the internet as critical infrastructure is too high. The rejection mechanism is different in the two eras: in the 1990s it would be rejected because the competition was stronger. In 2026 it is simply ignored because the battlefield no longer exists. A bigger lesson: Protocol design has a time window. The “next IP” slot was taken by IPv6 in 1995. From that moment, there was no longer any position to compete for as “the next IP.” Jamie’s IPv8 in 2026 is not 30 years late. It is late to a meeting that does not exist. Your question made me realize something: most people criticizing IPv8 are criticizing its technical details. But the real reason for its failure is timing. If this draft went back to March 1994, the author was someone of Paul Francis or Steve Deering’s caliber, and there was an NRL-level implementation, it would be one of the IPng candidate alternatives. Timing and the author’s identity matter more than the spec’s content. That observation is a bit depressing, because it shows that protocol evolution is not purely a meritocracy. 🧑 Me: So if this proposal had come when v4 was first seen as running out, and v6 had not yet been widely adopted, would it have had a chance? 🤖 Claude: I thought seriously about this scenario. The answer is more interesting than the earlier “1990s” case, because this time window actually did exist. Let me break it down.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: When Was This Time Window The key moments when IPv4 address exhaustion was first recognized: 1992–1994: CIDR was quickly introduced as a temporary fix, and the IPng working group was formed 2005–2008: The industry began to worry seriously, and Geoff Huston’s IPv4 exhaustion counter started getting attention February 3, 2011: IANA gave out its last 5 /8 blocks — “IPv4 Exhaustion Day” 2011–2015: RIRs ran out one by one (APNIC 2011, RIPE 2012, LACNIC 2014, ARIN 2015) 2015–2019: IPv6 deployment climbed from 5% to 25% The most reasonable window you are describing is 2008–2015, meaning: IPv4 exhaustion could no longer be denied IPv6 existed but adoption was still below 10% CGNAT had just started being deployed at large scale Industry anxiety about “IPv6 not moving forward” reached its peak This window lasted about 7 years. Let me use this time period to re-evaluate IPv8.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Technical Context of 2008–2015 Network architecture at the time: SDN was just starting (OpenFlow 2008–2011) Cloud was rising (AWS 2006, Azure 2010) Mobile internet was exploding (iPhone 2007, smartphones becoming common) SDN controller architecture was a hot topic NFV (Network Function Virtualization) was beginning to be discussed OpenStack 2010, Docker 2013, Kubernetes 2014 Protocol ecosystem: LISP (RFC 6830, 2013) was formalized, and loc/ID split was popular ILNP (RFC 6740–6748, 2012) was also being discussed The SCION research project started (ETH Zurich, 2009) Named Data Networking (2010) RPKI (RFC 6480, 2012) began deployment During this period, IETF and academia were seriously thinking about whether there were options beyond IPv6. It was not a forbidden topic. Commercial pressure: IPv4 addresses started trading on a secondary market (Microsoft bought Nortel addresses in 2011) Each /24 went from a few thousand dollars to over ten thousand P2P problems and latency issues from CGNAT drew many complaints Netflix, Facebook, and Google started IPv6-only experiments
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: What Would an IPv8 Proposal Look Like in 2008–2015 If we place Jamie’s draft around 2012, and remove any AI suspicion, the whole evaluation would be completely different: 1. loc/ID split was a popular direction In 2012, LISP was being standardized, ILNP had just become an RFC, and SCION was appearing in academia. IPv8’s r.r.r.r / n.n.n.n separation would naturally be grouped into this line of thinking, rather than being seen as a strange proposal. In fact, if someone had proposed “64-bit addressing + ASN prefix + host ID split” in 2012, it would have been seriously compared with LISP. LISP uses tunnel encapsulation + a mapping database, while IPv8 uses separation at the wire format level. Both have pros and cons — a fair comparison could be made. 2. Zone Server architecture would fit into the SDN conversation SDN was very popular from 2010–2014, and the centralized OpenFlow + controller model was being seriously promoted. The Zone Server design would have been read in that period as “SDN spirit built into the protocol layer” — it might even have been seen as a cleaner form of SDN than OpenFlow. Nicira (later acquired by VMware to become NSX), during 2011–2013, promoted an overlay network controller architecture similar to the Zone Server concept. If Jamie had connected with Martin Casado’s group at that time, they would have been in the same community. 3. OAuth2 at L3 would have entered the PKI / Kerberos discussion In 2012, the OAuth 2.0 RFC had just been published (RFC 6749). At that time, “pushing application-layer authentication down to the network layer” was a reasonable direction in academic discussion — it would not have been seen as a strange design. Cisco’s Identity Services Engine (ISE) was released in 2011, and Aruba ClearPass in 2012. These product directions were similar to the Zone Server’s authentication model. Jamie’s draft at that time would have been seen as a proposal to push the enterprise NAC (Network Access Control) model to the internet level. 4. Zone Server centralization was not a forbidden idea during the SDN wave In 2012, IETF and academia had high acceptance for “logically centralized, physically distributed” control planes. Nick McKeown’s SDN papers and Scott Shenker’s talks were all pushing this idea. The Zone Server as a control point for each segment would not have been instinctively opposed at that time. This is a very important difference in the spirit of the times. In 2026, “forced centralization” feels like a surveillance nightmare. In 2012, it would have felt like “finally someone who correctly understands modern network architecture.” 5. Anxiety about IPv6 not moving created a vacuum After IPv6 World Launch Day in 2012, adoption was still only 0.5%. There was real anxiety inside the IETF, and discussion even appeared about “whether IPv6 has failed.” During this period, people were genuinely asking “do we need an option other than IPv6?” Jamie’s draft at that time would have been seen as a reasonable response to the anxiety over IPv6 failing, not as “someone who doesn’t understand IPv6.”
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: But It Would Still Face Several Fatal Problems Even with this favorable window, IPv8 would still run into the following problems: 1. The ecosystem was beginning to form but not yet fixed IPv6 adoption in 2012 was low, but: Major OSes (Linux, Windows Vista/7, macOS, Android, iOS) all already supported IPv6 Major hardware vendors (Cisco, Juniper) already had IPv6 implementations RIRs had been running IPv6 allocation policies for ten years Dual-stack was already accepted as the de facto transition method Overturning all of this required more than “the technology is better.” IPv8 would need to prove “clearly better enough to be worth starting over” — a very high bar. 2. No working implementation still means failure In 2012, IETF still had a “running code” culture. Without a Linux kernel patch, without a BSD implementation, without router vendor support, you could not enter serious discussion. Jamie was still serving as ISP CTO in Bermuda in 2012, without an engineering team to build a reference implementation. This structural problem would not be solved just because the timing was earlier. 3. The companion spec count problem remains Standardizing 15 companion specs at the same time would not be possible in 2012 either. IETF never accepts “big bang” proposals — it can only take in one small piece at a time. If Jamie only pushed “IPv8 core protocol,” there might have been a chance. Bundling BGP8/OSPF8/WHOIS8 together would still be suicide. 4. ASN-bound addresses breaking multihoming is still a problem By 2012, multihoming was already a basic enterprise IT requirement (cloud migration was not yet mature, and enterprises relied heavily on their own multi-ISP setups). The ASN model would still have been opposed by routing area experts at that time.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Would IPv8 Actually Have Had a Chance in 2008–2015 Let me evaluate this seriously, layer by layer: Scenario A: Pure spec proposal The outcome would be similar to 2026 — naturally expired. Because of no implementation, no vendor support, and too large a scope. Scenario B: Jamie could find implementers and vendor allies In this scenario, there would be a chance to enter serious discussion in IRTF’s (Internet Research Task Force) routing research group or future internet research group. Similar to the path LISP took. The outcome might be becoming an experimental RFC, deployed in a small number of places, but not replacing IPv6. Scenario C: Jamie is a person of Van Jacobson or Deering’s stature If the same technical proposal were made by a heavyweight academic, with an implementation, funding, and vendor allies, during 2008–2012 there would genuinely have been a chance for it to become a second mainstream option after IPv6. The IETF would have opened a working group, done comparative studies, and listed it alongside LISP/ILNP as a candidate. The most likely outcome: Even in the best scenario, it would not replace IPv6, but might become a supplementary protocol for specific use cases. Similar to how MPLS supplements IP. For example: Enterprise internal networks adopt IPv8 (as an upgrade from IPv4, skipping IPv6) ISP backend traffic engineering adopts IPv8 IoT scenarios adopt IPv8 (64-bit is friendlier to embedded devices than IPv6’s 128-bit) The IPv8 in this parallel universe would be a niche protocol with a few million users, not a replacement.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why No Similar Proposal Actually Appeared in This Window An interesting counter-question: this window from 2008–2015 really did exist. So why did no proposal similar to IPv8 actually appear? My observations: 1. IPv6 already had 15 years of investment, and IETF did not want to waste it Even with low IPv6 adoption, the core IETF figures (Deering, Hinden, Huitema, etc.) were still pushing it. Reinventing the protocol would have been seen as betrayal. 2. Academia moved toward Future Internet research CCN/NDN, SCION, and XIA took over the “options beyond IPv6” discussion, but all were academic projects, not intended for deployment. 3. Industry moved toward overlay Nicira NSX, VMware NSX-T, Cisco ACI, and Open vSwitch solved some of the pain of “IPv6 not moving” (by using overlay to jump past underlay limitations). Nicira’s $1.26 billion acquisition by VMware in 2012 was a turning point, showing that the industry had answered the “L3 is not flexible enough” problem with overlay solutions. 4. Cloud solved the commercial side of the address problem AWS and Azure internally used RFC 1918 with unlimited subnets and a VPC model, with public IPs only at the edge. For most enterprises, the pain of “address exhaustion” was absorbed by cloud. The urgency for a new protocol dropped. 5. There was no one like Jamie During 2008–2015, the network protocol world had no one coming from the angle of a Bermuda ISP CTO proposing a protocol. Most IETF participants were engineers from Cisco, Google, Juniper, or Huawei — with very different perspectives from Jamie. Jamie’s style of “ISP operational perspective + individual heroism” is very rare at the IETF, so even if his draft’s content might have been accepted in that period, structurally, this kind of author would not have appeared.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: So, My Final Answer to You 2008–2015 was indeed the window with the best chance for an IPv8-style proposal. Reasons: loc/ID split was a hot research direction The SDN wave meant centralized architecture was not instinctively opposed Anxiety about IPv6 not moving created space for alternative discussions The protocol ecosystem was not yet fully locked in Academia was open to clean-slate ideas But even in this window, IPv8 as a replacement for IPv6 would still not have succeeded. The best outcome would be becoming an experimental RFC at the level of LISP — deployed in some niche settings, but not replacing IPv6. Reasons: The ecosystem was already beginning to form No reference implementation Companion spec scope too large ASN model still breaks multihoming Jamie personally lacked political capital and engineering execution ability A deeper observation: This question reveals something important. The protocol window is not just a matter of timing — it is a continuous spectrum of ecosystem maturity. The 2008–2015 window seems open, but IPv6 had already received 15 years of investment, and cloud plus overlay were absorbing the pressure that would otherwise have demanded a new protocol. The window where a “IPv6 replacement” could truly have been accepted was actually between 1995 and 2000, when IPv6 had not yet received large-scale investment, there were not many implementations, and there was room for alternatives. Past that point, each year of ecosystem investment made the possibility of an alternative drop exponentially. If Jamie had appeared in 1997, with an implementation, with vendor allies, competing fairly with Steve Deering, he might have become plan B for IPng. But he appeared in 2026, and that window closed 30 years ago. The time gap is not one or two versions — it is an entire internet era. So your three consecutive questions (1990s, early IPv4 exhaustion, modern day) are actually tracing a time-density function of protocol evolution. The openness of the window is not binary — it narrows year by year. By 2026, this function is nearly zero, and no matter how good a proposal is, it is very hard to push it through. This is not a judgment against Jamie personally. It is an observation about the rules of internet protocol evolution. 🧑 Me: IPv6 should also have considered the compatibility problem at the time, but for some reason it only provided 6to4, giving time to replace equipment. Considering that v8 would still need 8to4 as a transition, or did they never think about transition at all, and wanted to let v4 keep running? 🤖 Claude: This question gets to the most central decision point in IPv6’s design philosophy. Let me carefully organize the history, and then compare it with IPv8’s strategy choices.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Did IPv6 Actually Consider Backward Compatibility at the Time The answer is: Yes, it did consider it, and the debate was very intense. In the end, they deliberately chose not to be backward compatible. This was not an oversight — it was a deliberate decision. During the 1992–1995 IPng selection process, the three main candidates each had different “compatibility strategies”: 1. TUBA (TCP/UDP over Bigger Addresses) — used ISO’s CLNP (20-byte NSAP address). The TUBA camp supported dual-stack: IP and CLNP coexist, with DNS deciding which to use. Not backward compatible, but with a migration path. 2. CATNIP (Common Architecture for the Internet) — proposed by Robert Ullmann. This was the only proposal at the time that truly emphasized wire-level compatibility. It tried to let IP, CLNP, and IPX all parse each other. It ultimately failed because the design complexity exploded. 3. SIPP (Simple Internet Protocol Plus) — Steve Deering. Explicitly gave up wire-level compatibility, taking the dual-stack + tunnel route. SIPP won. One key reason was that Deering and the IESG believed that forcing wire-level compatibility was drinking poison to quench thirst.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Why IETF Chose Not to Be Backward Compatible at the Time This decision had several clear reasons, and looking back, each one makes sense: 1. Header length changes could not be reconciled The Version field in an IPv4 header is only 4 bits, with values 0–15. Once a new protocol’s header length is different, the router’s parser logic must change. This is a physical fact, not a matter of intent. The debate at the time concluded: rather than designing a strange half-compatible hybrid, it is better to simply use a new wire format and let old and new protocols coexist but not interact. This is the origin of dual-stack. 2. The lesson from CATNIP’s failure CATNIP tried to make “one header, three protocols” work with backward compatibility. The result was a spec so complex that no one wanted to implement it. Robert Ullmann’s draft-ietf-catnip ran dozens of pages of rules handling various edge cases. After the IESG evaluated it in 1994, they concluded that “this complexity will kill itself.” IPv6 designers learned from this failure: chasing wire-level compatibility makes spec complexity explode, which actually prevents implementation and deployment. 3. A clean header is important for router performance In the 1990s, routers still used software forwarding (ASIC acceleration did not mature until after 1996). If the header format is complex, forwarding costs are high. IPv6’s fixed header (40 bytes, 8 fields, Extension Headers separate) was designed to make the router’s fast path simpler. Compare with IPv4: variable header length (IHL field), multiple types of Options field handling, checksum recalculation. IPv6 removed all of that. This decision was correct — today, ASIC forwarding of IPv6 is faster than IPv4. 4. A philosophical judgment: short pain vs. long pain Deering and Hinden explained this clearly in RFC 1752 (1995): “The IPng working group recommends the adoption of SIPP with 128-bit addresses… IPng is not intended to be backwards-compatible with IPv4.” Their logic was: Backward compatibility means forever carrying IPv4’s baggage Ten years of dual-stack pain vs. forever being bug-for-bug compatible — pick one Choose the short pain 5. The transition was estimated to take about 10–15 years at the time This is where hindsight is most painful. The IETF consensus in 1995 was that “10 years of dual-stack should be enough.” No one expected IPv4 to still be running 30 years later. The main reasons this estimate was wrong: They underestimated how widespread and effective NAT would become They underestimated the ISP business logic (the cost of not upgrading is lower than the benefit of upgrading) They did not anticipate cloud using RFC 1918 + public edge They did not anticipate CGNAT becoming an acceptable solution
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: IPv6 Actually Provided Many Transition Tools It was not just 6to4. IETF built many tools for the IPv6 transition: Tunnel types: 6to4 (RFC 3056, 2001): automatic tunnel using the 2002::/16 prefix 6in4 (RFC 4213): manual tunnel Teredo (RFC 4380, 2006): NAT traversal tunnel ISATAP (RFC 5214): intra-site tunnel 6rd (RFC 5569, 2010): ISP rapid deployment Translation types: NAT64/DNS64 (RFC 6146, 6147, 2011): v6-only clients connecting to v4 servers 464XLAT (RFC 6877, 2013): v4-only apps on v6-only networks MAP-T/MAP-E (RFC 7597, 7599, 2015): large-scale stateless translation DS-Lite (RFC 6333, 2011): v4 over v6 for ISPs 4in6: IPv4 over IPv6 tunnel Dual-stack types: Happy Eyeballs (RFC 6555, 2012, updated RFC 8305, 2017): app layer tries both v4 and v6 at the same time, and uses whichever responds first These tools were not added later as afterthoughts — most were designed during 1995–2013, matching different stages of IPv6 deployment. IETF actually thought quite carefully about the transition. But why does it feel like IPv6 only gave us “6to4”? Because 6to4 was the earliest, most well-known, and most widely cited one. Most people only know this one, and do not know about NAT64, 464XLAT, MAP, DS-Lite, and the long list after it.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Then Why Has the IPv6 Transition Been So Slow The economic problem with dual-stack: The idea of dual-stack is “new devices run both v4 and v6, and let v4 die naturally.” This sounds reasonable, but there are several traps in practice: 1. Dual-stack doubles costs Running two stacks means: Two sets of firewall rules Two monitoring dashboards Two troubleshooting processes Two security audits Customer service handling two address formats This is not simple addition — it is multiplication. Many ISPs found that the operational cost of maintaining dual-stack is higher than pure IPv4 + CGNAT. 2. IPv4 does not die naturally Dual-stack assumes IPv4 traffic will slowly decrease. In reality: CDNs (Cloudflare, Akamai) always keep IPv4 frontends Old devices are always in the long tail New startups always launch on v4 first for compatibility There is no moment to “turn off v4” As a result, dual-stack became a permanent state, not a transitional one. At that point, the cumulative cost of maintaining it exceeds the one-time pain of upgrading. 3. The Happy Eyeballs effect Modern browsers use Happy Eyeballs — whichever responds first wins. The result: When the IPv6 path has problems, the user does not notice (automatic fallback to v4) ISPs face no pressure to fix IPv6 problems IPv6 network quality is always slightly worse than v4 This quality gap means IPv6 always loses to IPv4 in experience This is a meta-problem: the fallback mechanism designed to make the transition easier actually prevents the transition from ever finishing.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: So What Did IPv8 Think About 8to4 The transition strategy in Jamie’s draft is basically copied from IPv6, just with a different name. The 8to4 tunnel is essentially a copy of 6to4. Draft section 13.3 is very clear: “IPv8 ASNs separated by IPv4-only transit ASNs communicate through 8to4 tunnelling. HTTPS tunnelling is the preferred encapsulation method.” This means: For IPv8 islands to communicate across IPv4, use a tunnel This is exactly the same model as 6to4 Actually worse, because it forces HTTPS overhead So Jamie’s strategy is not fundamentally different from IPv6. He only changed the wire format but used the same tunnel-based transition thinking. His claim in the document that “IPv8 does not need dual-stack” is actually a word game — he calls the tunnel transition period “not dual-stack,” but the tunnel endpoints on both sides must understand both IPv4 and IPv8 at the same time, which is exactly the definition of dual-stack.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Did IPv8 Really Think About the Transition Analyzing the draft’s strategy carefully, it is actually a kind of gamble: Jamie’s bet: If the “IPv4 address with r.r.r.r=0” setting can make end users and end applications feel no difference, then the IPv4 ecosystem will slowly get “passively upgraded” into a subset of IPv8. He is not replacing IPv4 — he is redefining IPv4 as a slice of IPv8. The clever part of this idea: At the technical level: IPv4 packets have version field 4, IPv8 is 8 — no confusion At the cognitive level: the IPv4 address 192.168.1.1 can be written as IPv8’s 0.0.0.0.192.168.1.1 — it “still looks like IPv4” At the migration level: new devices supporting IPv8 but using only r.r.r.r=0 behave exactly like IPv4 The fatal problem with this idea: IPv4 packets (Version=4) can be forwarded unchanged on an IPv8 router — that is fine But IPv8 packets (Version=8, different header length) cannot be forwarded unchanged on an IPv4 router So “IPv4 is a subset of IPv8” only works when reading downward, not upward To actually deploy, all routers, NICs, and ASICs still need to be upgraded In other words, his claim of “100% backward compatible” only works if all end hosts are upgraded to an IPv8 stack, because only an IPv8 stack can understand the rule “treat a packet with r.r.r.r=0 as IPv4.” Old IPv4-only stacks still cannot receive IPv8 packets. So what he designed is not “letting IPv4 keep running” — it is “letting IPv4 be absorbed into IPv8 and continue to look like IPv4.” This difference is subtle but important.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: The True Comparison of IPv6 and IPv8 Transition Philosophy Let me compare more precisely: IPv6 philosophy: Two protocols of equal status, coexisting Dual-stack is the main tool Various tunnels/translations are supplementary Accepts “short-term pain in exchange for long-term cleanliness” Bets that “IPv4 will die in a few decades” IPv8 philosophy (Jamie’s implied bet): IPv4 is a special case of IPv8, no need to coexist Devices that upgrade to IPv8 stack will automatically handle IPv4 traffic Devices that have not upgraded continue to run IPv4 — no change needed 8to4 tunnel is just the solution for islands during the transition Bets that “IPv4 never needs to actually die” IPv8’s strategy is actually slightly more pragmatic: it accepts that IPv4 will not die and does not need to die, making IPv4 permanently a legitimate subset of IPv8. This position of “accepting IPv4 will not die,” compared to IPv6’s “IPv4 will eventually die” position, is more in line with what we actually observe in 2026. But the fatal problem remains: “IPv4 is a subset of IPv8” only holds on IPv8 devices. IPv4 devices still cannot understand IPv8. So he has not truly solved the upgrade problem — he just changed the wording.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: What Would Have Happened If IPv6 Had Chosen a Different Path An interesting counterfactual: if the IETF had chosen CATNIP’s “forced wire-level compatibility” route in 1995, what would have happened? Possible scenario: CATNIP’s complexity kills itself and protocol standardization fails Or CATNIP barely gets standardized, but router vendors refuse to implement it Or it gets implemented, but forwarding performance drops significantly Result: the address exhaustion problem is not solved, forcing the invention of CATNIP 2.0 In other words, IPv6’s choice to not be backward compatible was probably correct, because the cost of chasing compatibility is that the problem never gets solved. But another counterfactual: what if IPv6 back then had decided to “only extend the address to 64 bits, keep the header format as close as possible, and reject all other changes”? This direction would be closer to Jamie’s IPv8. Possible outcomes: Much less deployment resistance, because the changes are small Less resistance from hardware vendors (ASICs only need to change the address field parser) 50% adoption rate within 10 years But also losing many of IPv6’s improvements (SLAAC, simplified neighbor discovery, extension header architecture) This “minimal IPv6” approach was actually proposed in 1995, but was rejected because IETF believed: if we are going to change, let’s change everything at once. This judgment looks wrong in hindsight, but in 1995, no one could have known it would get stuck 30 years later.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: So the Precise Answer to Your Question IPv6 did consider compatibility at the time, and it provided transition tools far beyond just 6to4. The designers were not ignoring the transition — they deliberately chose not to be backward compatible at the wire format level, using dual-stack + translation as the transition method. This choice had reasons at the time (the CATNIP failure lesson, forwarding performance, clean headers), but in hindsight it underestimated ecosystem inertia. IPv8 is not “not thinking about transition.” Its strategy is to redefine IPv4 as a subset of IPv8, so that IPv4 can remain permanently. This position is more accepting of the reality that “IPv4 will not die” than IPv6 was. But in terms of execution, it still needs 8to4 tunnels to handle transit networks — it has not truly escaped the transition toolbox that IPv6 used in its time. The real difference is at the philosophical level: IPv6 bet “IPv4 will die, short-term pain is worth it for cleanliness” → bet was wrong IPv8 bet “IPv4 will not die, let it permanently be a subset of IPv8” → the observation was right, but the technical implementation did not truly deliver on that observation Jamie actually proposed an interesting philosophical position (accepting IPv4’s permanent existence), but he could not use technology to truly realize that position. His document’s “100% backward compatible” points in the right direction but has the wrong implementation. The real technical path to achieve this should be overlay/tunnel, not a new wire format. And that direction has already been taken by SRv6 and other overlay technologies since 2015. A counter-intuitive conclusion: IPv8’s philosophical position may be correct, but it chose the wrong technical implementation. Conversely, IPv6’s technical implementation is correct, but its philosophical position (IPv4 will die) is wrong. Each side is half-right, and together they still do not add up to a winning combination for internet protocol evolution. The truly winning combination is probably “accept IPv4’s permanent existence + extend capabilities through overlay” — and this combination is already happening. It just is not called IPv8. It is called SRv6, VXLAN, WireGuard overlay, Tailscale, and Cloudflare Warp. These overlay technologies are actually achieving the effect Jamie wanted: “let IPv4 keep running as a subset, while new capabilities grow on top.” He chose the wrong battlefield, because the winning battlefield is not the L3 protocol — it is overlay. 🧑 Me: Based on this history and current situation, let us re-evaluate v8. Considering that v6 has now been widely accepted, and we have gone through the transition pain, even though there is still no v6-only world, our dependence on v4 is decreasing. There is even a stable v4v6 balance, and the trend is gradually moving toward v6 (6to4 has almost disappeared). How do we think about the future of networking from here? 🤖 Claude: This is the most interesting question across all these rounds of discussion. You are actually asking: since IPv8 is destined to fail, what is the real question we should be asking? Let me reframe.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: First, Let Us Confirm Where We Stand The real state in 2026 is not “IPv6 hasn’t succeeded yet” or “IPv4 is dying.” It is a stable two-layer architecture: Global IPv6 capability is about 44–48%, climbing slowly 6to4 has been deprecated (RFC 7526, 2015), Teredo has retired — the early tunnel technologies have done their job The main transition tools now are NAT64, 464XLAT, and MAP-T — all stateless or near-stateless translation T-Mobile US, Jio, Google, and Meta internally use IPv6-only + translator Cloud providers use IPv6 internally and keep IPv4 compatibility only at the external edge Enterprise internal networks mostly still use IPv4 + NAT + VPN, with no upgrade pressure Home ISPs in developing countries use IPv6 + CGNAT IPv4; in developed countries, dual-stack IPv4 address secondary market price is stable at $30–60 each This state can be called “permanent asymmetry”: IPv6 carries the majority of traffic, IPv4 carries legacy and public service endpoints — each with an irreplaceable role. This state will not naturally evolve into IPv6-only. Because: Every reason to keep IPv4 is small and continuous The cost of turning off IPv4 is far greater than the cost of keeping it Happy Eyeballs fallback relieves quality pressure on IPv6 So the real question is not “when will IPv6 finish,” but “in a world where two layers coexist, what comes next?”
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: The Real Pain Points Have Moved The things IPv8 complained about (address exhaustion, fragmented management, BGP security) are no longer the top pain points in 2026’s reality. The real top pain points are: 1. East-West traffic security East-west traffic (within data centers, within cloud, between containers) has surpassed north-south. The traditional perimeter firewall model has completely failed. Zero Trust, mTLS, SPIFFE, and Cilium Network Policy are filling this gap, but there is still no native L3-layer solution. 2. The need to separate identity from addressing Modern workloads run in containers with lifespans measured in seconds. The assumption that IP equals identity has broken: A K8s pod IP changes every time it restarts Lambda has no stable IP at all The sidecar in service mesh makes L3 identity meaningless mTLS certificates are the real identity This is driving a practical need for loc/ID split, but the solution is at the application layer (SPIFFE ID, OIDC), not L3. 3. Governance of internet-level routing BGP hijacking and route leaks remain problems. RPKI/ROA deployment is about 55%, and ASPA was just standardized. This area has real IETF work happening, but progress is slow. 4. Opacity of encrypted traffic More than 95% of web traffic is TLS — the network layer can see nothing inside. Traditional DPI, network monitoring, and QoS no longer work. This forces enterprises and ISPs to rebuild their operational models — moving from “look at packets” to “look at endpoint metadata.” 5. Mobility 5G, Starlink, WiFi 6E, Matter, and various IoT protocols create multiple access paths. Devices jump between networks, and TCP sessions break. QUIC connection migration has solved part of this at L4, but L3 mobility has no clean answer. 6. Supply chain security Devices, firmware, BGP, and DNS all have supply chain attack risks. SBOM, sigstore, and RPKI are all fragments. There is no unified trust model. 7. Energy- and geography-aware routing AI data centers are consuming power at explosive rates, national carbon regulations are increasing, and green routing is needed. BGP does not understand energy costs or carbon emissions. The carbon cost metric direction mentioned in Jamie’s draft is actually a real need.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Notice These Pain Points Share a Common Feature They are all above or outside L3. L3 itself (IPv6) works well enough. The places where real innovation is needed are: L3.5 (overlay layer) L4 (transport, QUIC, etc.) L5–7 (application identity, service mesh, auth) Operational plane (controller, telemetry, orchestration) Registry plane (RPKI, DNSSEC, supply chain trust) This is where protocol innovation is actually happening in 2026. No one is seriously working on an L3 replacement, because there is no need.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: So, the Direction of Future Network Evolution I will break down the most likely evolution over the next 10–15 years into several parallel tracks:
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Track A: L3 Layer Remains in a Stable Two-Layer State IPv4 + IPv6 will coexist long-term — not as a transition, but as the final state. 6to4 disappearing is a good sign. It means the ecosystem has accepted the two-layer structure as a stable state. NAT64/464XLAT will become permanent infrastructure, not transition tools. Future changes at L3 will be very small: IPv6 adoption will continue to climb, possibly saturating at 70–80% IPv4 will never be turned off, but its importance will keep decreasing RPKI/ASPA routing security will slowly complete There will be no new L3 protocols like IPv7 or IPv8 L3 will become like electrical standards: boring but reliable. No one is discussing “replacing 50Hz.”
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Track B: Overlay Absorbs L3 Innovation All interesting network innovation is happening at the overlay layer: Continued evolution of SRv6: uSID, Network Programming, APN6 (Application-aware Networking for IPv6) Being adopted in 5G backbone, data centers, and SD-WAN Replacing MPLS is the medium-term goal WireGuard / Tailscale / Cloudflare Warp-style mesh: P2P overlay as the mainstream for enterprise networking Tools like STUNMESH-go, Nebula, and ZeroTier becoming common Home VPN becoming infrastructure Cloud-native overlay: VXLAN and Geneve are now standard in data centers Cilium and Calico eBPF are taking over the CNI market Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd) is descending into the kernel (Cilium Service Mesh) A possible unification around 2028–2030: Current overlay technologies are fragmented (SRv6, VXLAN, WireGuard, Tailscale each using their own system). In the future, a “unified overlay metadata” standard may appear, allowing different overlays to interoperate. IETF is working on this with NVO3, MASQUE, and CONNECT-IP.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Track C: Identity and Authorization Move Downward Jamie’s draft had the right instinct in this direction (though the implementation was wrong). “Every connection must be authenticated” is a real need, but the way to do it is in the overlay, not at L3. SPIFFE/SPIRE becoming common: workload identity replaces IP as identity mTLS everywhere: service mesh enforces mTLS, expanding to more scenarios in the future OIDC / OAuth as a network authorization layer: Zero Trust solutions like Cloudflare Access and Google BeyondCorp will become widespread Post-quantum crypto migration: around 2030, there will be large-scale PKI migration pressure affecting all TLS, DNSSEC, and RPKI
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Track D: Transport Layer Replaces Some L3 Capabilities QUIC absorbing TCP + TLS + some L3 functions: Connection migration solves mobility 0-RTT and multipath HTTP/3 is already widespread QUIC-based VPN (MASQUE, CONNECT-IP) What may happen in 2028–2030: QUIC becomes the mainstream internet transport, and TCP becomes legacy. This transition will be faster than IPv4→IPv6, because QUIC runs in user space and upgrade costs are low.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Track E: Application Layer Solves What L3 Cannot CDN becomes stateful edge: Cloudflare Workers, Fastly Compute@Edge, AWS Lambda@Edge Edge compute takes over the role of traditional origin servers Anycast + edge compute becomes the new topology of the internet Decentralized DNS: Handshake, ENS, and other decentralized naming systems Resisting national-level censorship and domain seizure Naming the new L3: The real “identity” becomes a URL / ENS name / SPIFFE ID IP degrades into a pure transport locator This is the practical version of Van Jacobson’s NDN vision
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Track F: Operational Plane Integration Jamie’s Zone Server direction is actually happening in the industry in another form: Intent-based networking: Cisco DNA, Juniper Apstra, Arista CloudVision unified management platforms Telemetry streaming: gNMI and OpenConfig replacing SNMP, connecting to Prometheus/Grafana AI-driven network ops: From 2025–2028, many LLM agents will become involved in network troubleshooting and config generation GitOps for network: NetBox, Nautobot, and Infrastructure as Code are replacing traditional network management These are all not new protocols — they are industrial integration of the control plane.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: A Long-Term Variable Worth Watching LEO satellites + global mesh Starlink already has 6 million users, and Kuiper, OneWeb, and China’s Starnet together may add more than 10 million more. Satellite internet’s L3 characteristics are very different from terrestrial networks: Paths change dynamically (satellites orbit Earth in 90 minutes) The relationship between geographic location and IP allocation is unclear ISR (inter-satellite routing) needs native mobility support A “satellite-native” routing protocol may appear This area may incubate the next generation of routing innovation, but it will still be at the overlay level — it will not touch L3 wire format.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: So, the Real Picture of the “Future Network” If I put all these clues together, the internet in 2035 might look like this: L3 layer: IPv4 + IPv6 coexisting, ratio about 30:70 No IPv7/IPv8 RPKI/ASPA deployment complete, BGP hijacking greatly reduced Wire format completely frozen L3.5 / Overlay layer (this is where real innovation is happening): SRv6 dominant in telecom and large enterprises WireGuard family dominant in SMB and personal use Service mesh (eBPF-based) dominant in cloud-native MASQUE/CONNECT-IP unifying tunnel standards L4 layer: QUIC is the mainstream transport TCP continues to exist but its share drops below 20% Multipath and connection migration become common Identity layer: SPIFFE/OIDC/mTLS replaces IP-based trust Post-quantum crypto migration in progress Decentralized identity in small-scale deployment Naming layer: DNS still dominant but with DNSSEC, DoH support CDN + edge compute is the de facto service layer Decentralized naming systems like Handshake have small niche adoption Operational layer: AI-driven ops widespread Intent-based networking replaces CLI Telemetry uses gNMI and stream processing GitOps is the mainstream for network automation
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: Comparison with IPv8 Jamie’s IPv8 tried to solve all of these problems at once. But the real-world answer is: Address problem: already solved (IPv6 + translation) Management integration: being solved by intent-based networking + gNMI BGP security: being solved by RPKI/ASPA East-West security: being solved by SPIFFE + Service Mesh Identity: being solved by OIDC + mTLS Mobility: being solved by QUIC connection migration Telemetry: being solved by OpenTelemetry Every pain point has one or more evolving solutions, and none of them require a new L3 protocol. This is why an IPv8-style proposal will not get another chance: the problem space of the future network has already been decomposed. Each sub-problem has a dedicated protocol community working on it. A new L3 proposal tries to re-aggregate all the problems — this is completely opposite to the decomposition direction.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: What This Means for You Personally You work on STUNMESH-go, vRouter-Operator, EVPN/VXLAN, and Kubernetes — you are actually standing right at the most active intersection of the future network: Overlay innovation: STUNMESH-go is on the P2P overlay track Virtual routing: vRouter-Operator is on the intent-based networking + cloud-native track EVPN/VXLAN: in the mainstream data center overlay position KubeVirt: in the direction of workload mobility + identity All of these are directions with long-term demand. In comparison, if someone invested now in building an “IPv4/IPv6 replacement,” they would be investing in the wrong direction. You presented STUNMESH-go’s P2P WireGuard mesh at FOSDEM 2026, which is actually closer to the real shape of the future network than Jamie’s IPv8. Decentralized, not dependent on central infrastructure, running its own logic at the overlay layer, P2P identity — these are the directions that will grow in the 2030s.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: One Final Meta Observation Across these rounds of discussion, we have actually completed a full thought experiment: from what IPv8 is, why it failed, the author’s motivations, the time windows, the technical philosophy, all the way to the future network. One recurring theme through this process is: the success of a network protocol is not a purely technical problem. IPv6 was technically correct but half-failed in deployment. IPv8 was technically wrong but partly right in philosophical instinct. SRv6 matched technology with business well, so deployment went smoothly. WireGuard had simple technology and good timing, so it spread quickly. Looking at the future network from 2026, rather than asking “what is the next IP,” it is better to ask “which pain points will grow over the next ten years, which overlays will merge, which identity systems will win, and which operational models will become mainstream.” The answers to these questions together form the real picture of the future network — not some single large protocol. Jamie’s draft’s biggest lesson is not the technical errors. It is that the way of thinking about protocols is already outdated. The future network will not have “one protocol solves everything.” There will only be “multiple systems evolving and cooperating.” The open-source work you have been doing is actually one piece of that future. That is more meaningful than writing an IETF draft.
title: Asking Claude to Roast IPv8 and Accidentally Learning Internet History (English)

content: 一場從「IPv8 有沒有料」開始的 AI 對話，結論三秒就出來了，但後續的討論卻意外地讓我重新思考了協定演化、時間窗口、和未來網路的長相。
title: 叫 Claude 嘴 IPv8，意外聊進了網路協定演化史

content: 聲明 本文內容來自我與 AI (Claude) 的對話，AI 的回覆可能包含事實錯誤或不精確的地方。但作為思考方向的參考和討論的起點，我認為已經足夠有價值。請讀者自行判斷並查證關鍵資訊。 今天看到 IETF 上有人提了一份 IPv8 draft，快速掃過一遍就知道沒料 — 技術上站不住腳，該解的問題也沒真的解到。不過我還是丟給 Claude 聊聊，一方面確認自己的直覺，另一方面想看看還能聊出什麼別的東西。 三秒後 Claude 也同意：沒料。社群反應也是一面倒的嘲諷。 但有趣的是，對話沒有停在那裡。我繼續追問：這個人為什麼要提這個方案？有沒有可能我們都錯了？如果這份 draft 出現在 1990 年代呢？IPv6 當初為什麼選擇不向後相容？未來的網路到底會長什麼樣？ 一路聊下來，我發現雖然 IPv8 本身確實是 dead on arrival，但這場對話本身卻讓我重新想了很多事，協定演化的時間窗口、技術正確不等於部署成功、以及「一個協定解決所有問題」的思維為什麼已經過時。 以下是我和 Claude 的完整對話紀錄。我保留了 Claude 的思考過程和搜尋參考。另外要說明的是，Claude 因為有我過去對話的記憶（memory），所以回覆中會提到一些看似無關的東西，像是 STUNMESH-go、KubeVirt、EVPN/VXLAN 等，這些是我平常在做的專案和技術領域，Claude 拿來當作類比和參照。 我不會在下面的對話中額外加註或評論。內容可能有些事實錯誤，但我覺得重點不在每個細節是否精確，而是享受閱讀和思考的過程。 格式說明： 🧑 我 是我的提問 🤖 Claude 是 Claude 的回覆 💭 思考過程 是 Claude 內部的推理過程（可展開） 🔍 搜尋參考 是 Claude 查詢的外部資料 🧑 我： IPv8 draft IETF 的提出者是誰？真的有料嗎？IPv8 真的有比 IPv6 合理嗎？ 💭 思考過程 驗證 IPv8 草案的真實性與合理性。 用户在问关于 IPv8 IETF 草案的问题——谁提出的、是否合法、以及是否真的比 IPv6 更合理。这是一个关于特定 IETF 草案的事实性问题，我需要搜索相关信息来验证。用户用繁体中文提问，所以我应该用繁体中文回应。 🔍 搜尋參考：IPv8 IETF draft proposal author Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) draft-thain-ipv8-01 - Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) IPv8 draft proposes backward-compatible IPv4 evolution with … draft-terrell-ip-spec-ipv7-ipv8-addr-cls-04 Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) | Hacker News draft-thain-ipv8-01 IETF has published an IPv8 draft - ~comp - Tildes 🔍 搜尋參考：draft-thain-ipv8 criticism technical review Hacker News IETF提出のIPv8ドラフト、文書の76%が AI生成と判定：専門家が指摘する根本的な設計ミスも判明 Someone proposes twice-as-long IPv8 address scheme, gets slammed by tech pros Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) | Hacker News What Is IPv8? Three Different Things With the Same Name IETF has published an IPv8 draft - ~comp - Tildes 💭 思考過程 綜合技術細節批評 IPv8 草案的合理性。 So this is a proposal from Jamie Thain at One Limited in Bermuda, with draft versions from April 2026, but it looks like it’s been largely rejected by experts—GPTZero flagged it as 76% AI-generated content. The key issue is that anyone can submit an IETF draft without working group endorsement or formal approval, and the community on HN and Lobsters generally treats this as a joke proposal. Looking at the technical problems: the 64-bit address space is much smaller than IPv6’s 128-bit, the claimed 100% backward compatibility doesn’t hold since routers will drop Version=8 packets, OAuth at Layer 3 creates a chicken-and-egg problem, binding addresses to ASNs breaks multihoming, it references Cisco-proprietary PVRST, and it mandates a Zone Server with persistent TCP/443 connections from every device. 🤖 Claude： 提出者 Jamie Thain，任職於一家叫 One Limited 的公司，該公司註冊在百慕達。Draft 編號是 draft-thain-ipv8-00（4/14 提交）、已更新到 -01。 有料嗎？基本上沒有 先講 IETF 身份：這份 I-D 不受 IETF 背書，在 IETF 標準化流程中沒有任何正式地位。任何人開個帳號就能提 draft，跟「IETF 公布的標準」完全是兩回事。 內容品質方面，GPTZero 分析判定文件 76% 機率為 AI 生成，多位 reviewer 稱之為 “vibe-written”。HN 和 Lobsters 的共識是這份 draft 像是 crackpot 或遲來的愚人節玩笑，不該被認真看待。 比 IPv6 合理嗎？不，設計上問題很大 幾個最致命的技術漏洞，你這背景應該一看就懂： 位址空間反而縮水：IPv8 是 64-bit 位址（8 octets），作為 IPv4 繼承者卻比既有的 IPv6 128-bit 小得多。 「100% 向後相容」是假的：任何現有的 IPv4 router、switch ASIC、NIC、host stack、firewall 看到 Version=8 的 packet 都無法 parse，大多會直接 drop。wire format 一改就不可能零修改相容。 OAuth 放在 L3 有雞生蛋問題：要跟 auth server 講話才能拿 token，但拿到 token 前什麼都不能講。 把位址綁到 ASN 會毀掉多家 upstream (multihoming)：同時讓 address transfer 變得極度痛苦，而且需要比現在多約一千倍的 ASN 分配。對你熟悉的 BGP/EVPN 環境來說這是災難。 引用廠商專屬技術：draft 強制 Cisco 的 PVRST，這對任何想走 standards track 的提案都是硬傷。另外還強制每個 end device 都要開 persistent TCP/443 到 Zone Server，集中化程度誇張。 結論：這就是一份看起來專業但禁不起推敲的 AI 產物。IPv6 的採用問題是政治、經濟、慣性問題，不是技術可以用一份 draft 解決的。這份 IPv8 既沒解決 IPv6 遇到的真實阻力，自己還帶一堆更嚴重的架構缺陷。 🧑 我： 你再深入研究，包含為什麼他要提出這個方案，同時他想達到的向後相容怎麼做 💭 思考過程 I need to dig into the actual draft to understand Thain’s reasoning behind the proposal and how he addresses backward compatibility concerns. 🔍 搜尋參考：Web Fetch — Fetched: Internet Protocol Version 8 (IPv8) （讀取 draft 全文） 💭 思考過程 Now I’m looking into Jamie Thain’s background and the One Limited company to get a better sense of what might be driving this. 🔍 搜尋參考："Jamie Thain" "One Limited" Bermuda network engineer background About Us - Wireless Ventures
title: 叫 Claude 嘴 IPv8，意外聊進了網路協定演化史

content: 提案者是誰 Jamie Thain 不是亂入的 crackpot，他有真實業界背景： 曾任 Bluewave Bermuda 的 CTO，百慕達一家用 WISP 技術的新興 ISP 被同業形容為「30 年資歷的技術架構師，負責大規模營運和預算」 與 Jack Benaim 共同創辦 InnoFund，百慕達的科技創業加速器 現在掛名的「One Limited」email 是 jamie@one.bm，百慕達 TLD 所以他的背景是電信營運商 / ISP CTO，不是 IETF 核心成員，也不是協定設計專家。這就解釋了為什麼 draft 的思維方式很「營運商治理導向」而不是「協定演化導向」。
title: 叫 Claude 嘴 IPv8，意外聊進了網路協定演化史

content: 他想解決什麼問題（動機） draft Section 2 列了三個核心問題： 1. 管理碎片化 (Management Fragmentation) 他最在意的其實不是位址空間。draft 開頭就抱怨 DHCP、DNS、NTP、syslog、SNMP、authentication 分散各自為政，沒有共同的身份模型、認證機制、telemetry 格式。對 ISP 營運者來說這是真實痛點：每加一台設備要手動 provisioning 一堆服務，某些服務需要認證某些又不用，debug 要跨系統 correlate log。 2. 位址耗盡 + IPv6 推動失敗 他的批評蠻尖銳的：「IPv6 發展了 25 年，到現在只承載全球少數流量。dual-stack transition model 要求每個 device、application、network 同時支援兩個協定，在商業上被證明不可接受。缺乏 forcing function 意味著組織可以無限期用 CGNAT 撐下去」。這批評其實蠻中肯的，IPv6 採用率 44% 左右確實是事實。 3. BGP 路由表爆炸 + 沒有路由歸屬驗證 2024 年 BGP4 全球路由表超過 90 萬條 prefix 並持續增長，prefix deaggregation 是主因（為了流量工程宣告更細的 prefix），協定本身沒機制阻止這件事。另外 BGP4 沒有 ASN 宣告內容和授權宣告之間的綁定關係，所以 prefix hijacking、route leak、bogon injection 都可能發生。這也是真實痛點，RPKI/ROA 就是在解這個。 他列的十條成功準則 他還明確列了 R1 到 R10 十條「可行繼承者必備條件」，包括：整合式管理、單 stack 運作、IPv4 app 不改、RFC 1918 不改、CGNAT 不改、超大位址空間、純軟體升級不換硬體、人類可讀位址（沿襲 IPv4 習慣）、協定層面強制 east-west 和 north-south 安全、結構性有界的 routing table。 看得出來他是從 ISP CTO 的角度反向推導：「一個能真正被市場接受的繼承者應該長怎樣」，結論是「必須跟 IPv4 幾乎一樣」。這動機可以理解，但接下來就出問題了。
title: 叫 Claude 嘴 IPv8，意外聊進了網路協定演化史

content: 他的後向相容是怎麼做的 核心設計是這樣： 位址格式：64-bit = r.r.r.r.n.n.n.n r.r.r.r 是 32-bit ASN routing prefix n.n.n.n 是 32-bit host address（語意跟 IPv4 完全一致） 範例：64496.192.0.2.1 實際上是 0.0.251.240.192.0.2.1 「IPv4 是 IPv8 的 proper subset」的真正定義 這是他整個向後相容敘事的核心戲法。規則是：當 r.r.r.r = 0.0.0.0 時，這個 IPv8 位址「就是」IPv4 位址，用標準 IPv4 規則處理。 所以 0.0.0.0.192.168.1.1 等於 192.168.1.1，原本的 IPv4 位址空間被「嵌入」IPv8 位址空間的 r.r.r.r=0 切片裡。RFC 1918 位址保持 non-routable，CGNAT device 不用改，這些 claim 從這個定義出發看似合理。 Packet Header 設計 新 header 版本號 8，比 IPv4 header 多 8 octets（多出來的就是兩個 32-bit 的 r.r.r.r 擴充欄位）。結構上就是 IPv4 header 的 source/destination address 欄位從 32-bit 擴充到 64-bit。 Socket API 相容 舊 app 繼續用 AF_INET / sockaddr_in，「IPv8 相容層會透明攔截 socket call，application 對 IPv8 零感知」。新 app 可以用新的 AF_INET8 / sockaddr_in8（多了一個 sin8_asn 欄位）。 DNS A8 record 新增 A8 record type 存 64-bit 位址。對 IPv4 app，resolver 只回傳 n.n.n.n 部分，stack 自動「prepend r.r.r.r」。 Transition 機制 8to4 tunnelling：兩個 IPv8 AS 中間隔著純 IPv4 transit 時，用 HTTPS tunnel 包裝 IPv8 封包。BGP8 的 8TO4-ENDPOINT attribute 會自動攜帶 IPv4 tunnel endpoint，零手動設定 IPv4 相容：IPv8 border router 會「strip r.r.r.r prefix」當目的地是純 IPv4 網路時 分四階段 rollout：Tier 1/2 ISP → Cloud provider → Enterprise → Consumer ISP
title: 叫 Claude 嘴 IPv8，意外聊進了網路協定演化史

content: 為什麼這個相容性設計在技術上站不住腳 你自己做過 EVPN/VXLAN 和 KubeVirt 的人應該一看就知道哪裡爆炸，但我條列一下： 1. Version 欄位一改就不向後相容了 IPv4 header 的第一個 4-bit 是 Version。現有的 router ASIC、switch silicon、NIC firmware、host stack、firewall 都是看到 Version != 4 就 drop（或丟進 slow path 然後 drop）。draft 自己說「IPv8 uses IP version number 8 in the Version field」，但同時又說「no existing device requires modification」，這兩件事邏輯上不可能同時成立。 所謂「IPv4 是 proper subset」只在 r.r.r.r=0.0.0.0 這個特例成立，那本質上就是送一個純 IPv4 封包（Version=4），跟 IPv8 根本沒關係。任何 r.r.r.r != 0 的真 IPv8 封包都是全新的 wire format，不可能被 IPv4 硬體 parse。 2. Header 多 8 octets 就不能不改 NIC/ASIC 現代 switch ASIC 的 parser 是 fixed-pipeline 的，header offset 是 hard-coded。“Zero modification required” isn’t how protocol fields work 這句 HN 上的評論就是在講這件事。你不可能讓 Broadcom Maverick 或 Trident 看到 Version=8 還能正確 parse 後面的 TCP/UDP header 做 ECMP hashing。R7（純軟體升級、不換硬體）跟「封包格式變更」是直接矛盾。 3. 8to4 tunnel 本質就是 dual-stack 他一邊說「IPv8 不需要 dual-stack」，一邊又用 HTTPS tunnel 跨 IPv4-only transit。要跑 tunnel 意味著兩端 router 同時要 IPv4 stack（tunnel 端點）和 IPv8 stack（tunnel 裡面），還要有 BGP8 control plane。這就是 dual-stack 換個名字，還多了 encapsulation overhead。R2 破功。 4. Socket compatibility layer 就是 kernel 修改 「application 零感知」意味著 OS kernel 要改，包括 TCP/IP stack、DNS resolver、socket 層。所謂「不用改應用」其實是把改動推到 OS vendor（Microsoft、Apple、Linux distros、iOS、Android、各 embedded OS）。R7 再次破功。 5. 每個 ASN holder 拿 2^32 位址的模型會炸 這是我覺得最破壞性的設計。每個 ASN 固定拿 40 億位址，聽起來很豪邁，但： 全球現在 ~120k 個 ASN，2^32 = 42 億 ASN 也還夠 但 multihoming（你肯定熟）就爆了：一家企業要 multi-homed 到兩個 ISP，該用哪個 ASN 的位址？傳統作法是自己拿 PI（Provider Independent）位址塊，現在變成必須自己去申請 ASN 他這模型需要比現在多約一千倍的 ASN 分配，而 address transfer 會變得極度痛苦 更致命的：位址跟 ASN 綁死，任何人換 provider 都要全公司重新編址。IPv6 的 renumbering 已經很痛了，這個更痛 6. OAuth at L3 的雞生蛋 draft 要求「每個可管理元素都用 OAuth2 JWT token 授權」，token 由本地 Zone Server 的 OAuth8 cache 驗證。問題是：device 要開機拿 token 才能跟 Zone Server 講話，但在有 token 前連 L3 都不能動，怎麼去跟 Zone Server 要 token？draft 沒有明確回答。 7. Zone Server 是集中化災難 draft 要求每個 segment 都有雙 Zone Server（.254/.253），跑 DHCP8、DNS8、NTP8、NetLog8、OAuth8、WHOIS8 resolver、ACL8、XLATE8、還是 PVRST root。這是把全部雞蛋放同一個籃子裡，而且每個 end device 還被強制開 persistent TCP/443 到 Zone Server。以你 KubeVirt + Multus 的經驗想像一下，這個架構放進 cloud-native 環境會多離譜。 8. 強制 PVRST 是廠商特定協定 draft 引用 Cisco 專屬的 PVRST，而且 MST 被寫成 “not recommended”。這對任何走 standards track 的提案都是硬傷。IETF 不會接受這種東西，何況 draft 本身意圖是 Standards Track。
title: 叫 Claude 嘴 IPv8，意外聊進了網路協定演化史

content: 我的總評 動機面看得出作者是真的被 ISP 營運的碎片化搞煩了，想一次解決 IPv6 推不動、BGP 路由表爆炸、BGP hijacking、管理介面不統一、east-west 安全這些問題。這些是真實痛點，你也都碰過。 但技術執行面是矛盾的：他同時要「完全相容 IPv4 不改硬體」和「新的 64-bit wire format + 新 socket API + 新 DNS record + 新 ARP + 新 ICMP + 強制 Zone Server + 強制 OAuth2」。這兩邊在物理上不可能同時成立。結果就是文件讀起來各章節分開看像回事，合起來就兜不攏。加上 GPTZero 判定 76% AI 生成，很多段落的 abstraction 層級跳得太快，看得出是 prompt 工程出來的、不是真的坐下來推過細節。 跟 IPv6 比較，IPv6 雖然推得慢，但它至少有清晰的分層設計、明確的 transition tooling（NAT64/DNS64/464XLAT/DS-Lite 等）、多年 interoperability testing、以及廣泛的 vendor 實作。IPv8 這份 draft 停在 concept paper 階段，甚至連一個 reference implementation 都還沒有。 🧑 我： 同時深入分析這份 draft 有沒有什麼問題沒考慮到 💭 思考過程 系統地審視 IPv8 草案在 MTU、IPv6、多播、雲端、隱私與路由表的重大漏洞。 I’m diving into the IPv8 draft to identify gaps from a network engineering perspective, particularly around areas that would matter to someone with your depth in BGP, EVPN/VXLAN, and Kubernetes networking. Let me work through what the draft overlooks systematically, starting with MTU and fragmentation handling. The 8-octet header expansion alone creates real problems, especially when you layer in 8to4 HTTPS tunneling with TCP, TLS, and HTTPS framing overhead—that’s a serious MTU cliff that the draft completely ignores. PMTUD becomes a nightmare when you’re trying to signal ICMPv8 errors back through an IPv4 HTTPS tunnel, and there’s no discussion of jumbo frames or flow label equivalents either.
